16	
17	// Wire model (requirement-6 groundwork — no plaintext magic bytes, no fixed
18	// offsets). Every frame on the wire is:
19	//
20	//	nonce[nonceLen] || obf(body) [ || tag[tagLen] ]
21	//
22	//   - nonce is nonceLen fresh random bytes (high entropy, never constant).
23	//   - body is (kind byte || kind-specific header || opaque payload). It is
24	//     obfuscated by XOR with an XChaCha20 keystream derived from a PSK-bound
25	//     obfuscation subkey and the per-frame nonce, so the type discriminant and
26	//     header never sit at a constant offset — a byte-histogram over many random
27	//     encodings shows no constant byte position.
28	//   - tag is present ONLY for authenticated kinds (CONTROL, PROBE): an
29	//     Encrypt-then-MAC HMAC-SHA256 (truncated to tagLen) over nonce||obf(body)
30	//     keyed by a distinct PSK-bound auth subkey. Decode verifies it and rejects
31	//     tampered or PSK-mismatched frames. DATA and PARITY are unauthenticated by
32	//     design (DoS-grade forgery accepted — the inner WireGuard layer
33	//     authenticates the real payload); they carry no tag but are still
34	//     keystream-obfuscated so the no-fixed-offset property holds and the peer,
35	//     sharing the PSK, derives the same keystream to decode them.
36	//
37	// The two subkeys are derived from the PSK with HKDF-SHA256 under distinct info
38	// labels, so the obfuscation stream and the authentication MAC never share key
39	// material. All primitives are vetted (crypto/hkdf, x/crypto/chacha20,
40	// crypto/hmac) — no hand-rolled crypto.
41	//
42	// The outer-seq DATA field is this codec's OWN sequence space (see
43	// docs/p0-findings.md §6); it is never the inner WireGuard counter, and this
44	// codec never inspects the opaque WG payload (§4).
45	
46	const (
47		// nonceLen is the XChaCha20 nonce length carried in the clear at the head of
48		// every frame.
49		nonceLen = chacha20.NonceSizeX // 24
50		// tagLen is the truncated HMAC-SHA256 authentication tag length appended to
51		// authenticated frames.
52		tagLen = 16
53		// subkeyLen is the length of each HKDF-derived subkey.
54		subkeyLen = 32
55	
56		infoObf  = "wanbond outer-frame obfuscation v1"
57		infoAuth = "wanbond outer-frame authentication v1"
58	)
59	
60	// DataOverhead is the number of bytes a KindData frame adds on top of its opaque
61	// payload on the wire: the clear nonce plus the DATA header (kind || outer-seq ||
62	// path-id || fec-group || fec-index || flags). DATA frames are unauthenticated
63	// (see the wire model above), so they carry no tag; this figure is therefore exact
64	// and is what the multipath Bind subtracts from the path MTU when sizing the inner
65	// tunnel (see internal/bind mtu.go). The fec-index byte is carried on every DATA
66	// frame — 0 and inert when FEC is disabled (T24) — so the codec's wire layout is
67	// invariant to the FEC toggle.
68	const DataOverhead = nonceLen + // clear nonce
69		1 + // kind discriminant
70		8 + // outer-seq (uint64)
71		1 + // path-id (uint8)
72		4 + // fec-group (uint32)
73		1 + // fec-index (uint8): shard position within the FEC group (T24)
74		1 // flags (uint8)
75	
76	// ParityOverhead is the number of bytes a KindParity frame adds on top of its
77	// shard payload on the wire: the clear nonce plus the PARITY header (kind ||
78	// fec-group || parity-index || data-count || path-id). PARITY, like DATA, is
79	// unauthenticated and carries no tag, so this figure is exact. The multipath Bind
80	// uses it to size the FEC parity-overhead MTU penalty (T24): a full-size parity
81	// frame is 5 bytes larger on the wire than a full-size DATA frame carrying the same
82	// inner payload, so with FEC enabled the inner MTU is reduced so BOTH fit the path
83	// MTU without fragmentation (see internal/bind mtu.go).
84	const ParityOverhead = nonceLen + // clear nonce
85		1 + // kind discriminant
86		4 + // fec-group (uint32)
87		2 + // parity-index (uint16)
88		1 + // data-count (uint8)
89		1 // path-id (uint8)
90	
91	// Kind is the outer frame discriminant. Values are nonzero so a zeroed buffer
92	// never decodes to a valid kind.
93	type Kind uint8
94	
95	const (
96		// KindData wraps one opaque WireGuard datagram with multipath/FEC metadata.
97		KindData Kind = 1
98		// KindParity carries an FEC parity symbol for a fec-group.
99		KindParity Kind = 2
100		// KindProbe is an authenticated path-probe (RTT / liveness) frame.
101		KindProbe Kind = 3
102		// KindControl is an authenticated out-of-band control frame.
103		KindControl Kind = 4
104	)
105	
106	func (k Kind) valid() bool {
107		return k == KindData || k == KindParity || k == KindProbe || k == KindControl
108	}
109	
110	// authenticated reports whether frames of this kind append (and verify) a MAC.
111	func (k Kind) authenticated() bool {
112		return k == KindProbe || k == KindControl
113	}

115	// Frame is the closed sum over the four outer frame kinds. The unexported
116	// marker method prevents other packages from adding kinds.
117	type Frame interface {
118		// Kind returns the frame's discriminant.
119		Kind() Kind
120		// appendBody appends the plaintext body (kind byte || header || payload).
121		appendBody(dst []byte) []byte
122		isFrame()
123	}

125	// Data wraps a single opaque WireGuard datagram. OuterSeq is this codec's own
126	// sequence space (never the inner WG counter); PathID/FECGroup/FECIndex/Flags feed
127	// the multipath scheduler (T12), FEC (T14/T24), and resequencer (T18). FECIndex is
128	// the data shard's position within its FEC group (0..M-1); it is 0 and inert when
129	// FEC is disabled. The receiver reconstructs the FEC data-shard bytes as
130	// OuterSeq || Payload, so a shard recovered from parity carries its own outer-seq
131	// (T24) — no separate per-group base state is needed to resequence a recovered
132	// frame.
133	type Data struct {
134		OuterSeq uint64
135		PathID   uint8
136		FECGroup uint32
137		FECIndex uint8
138		Flags    uint8
139		Payload  []byte
140	}
141	
142	// Parity carries one FEC parity symbol for a fec-group. DataCount is the group
143	// cardinality M (the number of data shards the parity protects); the decoder learns
144	// M from any surviving parity frame, so it must ride the wire (T24).
145	type Parity struct {
146		FECGroup    uint32
147		ParityIndex uint16
148		DataCount   uint8
149		PathID      uint8
150		Payload     []byte
151	}
152	
153	// Probe is an authenticated path probe used for RTT and liveness estimation.
154	//
155	// IsEcho is the request/response discriminant. A probe emitted by an originator
156	// (telemetry.Prober.SendProbe) carries IsEcho=false; the responder reflects it
157	// verbatim with IsEcho=true (telemetry.Reflector.Reflect). The two are otherwise
158	// byte-for-body-identical, so the receiving transport needs this bit to route an
159	// inbound frame: IsEcho=false is a peer probe to REFLECT, IsEcho=true is an echo
160	// of our own probe to FEED into that path's Prober. Marking the echo also breaks
161	// the otherwise-unbounded reflect-of-a-reflect loop (an echo is never reflected).
162	//
163	// SessionID is the originator's random per-boot session identity (T38, defect
164	// D12). It sits inside the MAC-covered body (adjacent to IsEcho), so an attacker
165	// can neither forge nor flip it. The responder reflects it verbatim. It TAGS the
166	// session (which boot the probe stream belongs to); on its own it is NOT proof of
167	// freshness (a captured probe carries a "never-seen" SessionID too), so the
168	// session-epoch reset is gated on the Challenge below, never on the SessionID
169	// merely being novel.
170	//
171	// Challenge is the responder-contributed freshness token (T38 redesign, defect
172	// D12). Like SessionID it lives inside obf(body) under the MAC, so it is both
173	// confidential (only a PSK holder can read it) and unforgeable. Its meaning is
174	// keyed by IsEcho:
175	//
176	//   - On an ECHO (IsEcho=true, emitted by the Reflector): Challenge is the
177	//     responder's CURRENT per-path issued challenge — a fresh random value the
178	//     originator must echo back to prove liveness.
179	//   - On a PROBE (IsEcho=false, emitted by the originator): Challenge is the last
180	//     issued challenge the originator learned from an echo (zero before it has
181	//     seen any). The responder adopts a new session epoch (resetting its
182	//     anti-replay high-water) ONLY when this equals its live issued challenge, so
183	//     a replayed probe — which can never carry the responder's current challenge —
184	//     can never seize the epoch or lock out the live peer.
185	type Probe struct {
186		PathID         uint8
187		ProbeSeq       uint64
188		TimestampNanos int64
189		IsEcho         bool
190		SessionID      uint64
191		Challenge      uint64
192		Payload        []byte
193	}
194	
195	// Control is an authenticated out-of-band control frame.
196	//
197	// Seq is a strictly-monotonic per-(peer, ControlType) sequence number: the
198	// freshness material the STATEFUL control-handling layer (telemetry.ControlGuard)
199	// uses to reject replays of a security-relevant control message (defect D4). The
200	// codec (Decode) verifies only the PSK HMAC and keeps no per-peer state, so a
201	// passively-captured valid control frame — e.g. a rekey — replays with a passing
202	// MAC; the downstream guard tracks a per-type high-water on Seq and drops the
203	// replay. Seq rides INSIDE obf(body) under the MAC (like Probe.ProbeSeq), so an
204	// attacker can neither read it nor forge/advance it to smuggle a replay past the
205	// guard.
206	type Control struct {
207		ControlType uint8
208		Seq         uint64
209		Payload     []byte
210	}
211	
212	func (Data) isFrame()    {}
213	func (Parity) isFrame()  {}
214	func (Probe) isFrame()   {}
215	func (Control) isFrame() {}
216	
217	func (Data) Kind() Kind    { return KindData }
218	func (Parity) Kind() Kind  { return KindParity }
219	func (Probe) Kind() Kind   { return KindProbe }
220	func (Control) Kind() Kind { return KindControl }
221	
222	func (f Data) appendBody(dst []byte) []byte {
223		dst = append(dst, byte(KindData))
224		dst = binary.BigEndian.AppendUint64(dst, f.OuterSeq)
225		dst = append(dst, f.PathID)
226		dst = binary.BigEndian.AppendUint32(dst, f.FECGroup)
227		dst = append(dst, f.FECIndex)
228		dst = append(dst, f.Flags)
229		return append(dst, f.Payload...)
230	}
231	
232	func (f Parity) appendBody(dst []byte) []byte {
233		dst = append(dst, byte(KindParity))
234		dst = binary.BigEndian.AppendUint32(dst, f.FECGroup)
235		dst = binary.BigEndian.AppendUint16(dst, f.ParityIndex)
236		dst = append(dst, f.DataCount)
237		dst = append(dst, f.PathID)
238		return append(dst, f.Payload...)
239	}
240	
241	func (f Probe) appendBody(dst []byte) []byte {
242		dst = append(dst, byte(KindProbe))
243		dst = append(dst, f.PathID)
244		dst = binary.BigEndian.AppendUint64(dst, f.ProbeSeq)
245		dst = binary.BigEndian.AppendUint64(dst, uint64(f.TimestampNanos))
246		dst = append(dst, boolByte(f.IsEcho))
247		dst = binary.BigEndian.AppendUint64(dst, f.SessionID)
248		dst = binary.BigEndian.AppendUint64(dst, f.Challenge)
249		return append(dst, f.Payload...)
250	}
251	
252	// boolByte encodes a bool as a single wire byte (0 or 1).
253	func boolByte(b bool) byte {
254		if b {
255			return 1
256		}
257		return 0
258	}
259	
260	func (f Control) appendBody(dst []byte) []byte {
261		dst = append(dst, byte(KindControl))
262		dst = append(dst, f.ControlType)
263		dst = binary.BigEndian.AppendUint64(dst, f.Seq)
264		return append(dst, f.Payload...)
265	}
266	
267	// ErrMalformed is returned by Decode for any input too short or structurally
268	// invalid to be a frame.
269	var ErrMalformed = errors.New("frame: malformed input")
270	
271	// ErrAuth is returned by Decode when an authenticated frame fails its MAC check
272	// (tampered ciphertext or PSK mismatch).
273	var ErrAuth = errors.New("frame: authentication failed")
274	
275	// Codec is a reusable, PSK-bound frame encoder/decoder. It derives the HKDF
276	// obfuscation and authentication subkeys ONCE at construction and reuses them
277	// (plus per-call scratch buffers) across every Encode/Decode, and it inits the
278	// XChaCha20 keystream exactly once per frame. This resolves defect D5: the old
279	// package-level Encode/Decode re-derived both subkeys with HKDF-SHA256 and
280	// double-init'd ChaCha20 (once in peekByte, once for the full body) on EVERY
281	// frame — prohibitive on the per-datagram datapath the multipath Bind drives.
282	//
283	// A Codec is NOT safe for concurrent use: its scratch buffers are shared across
284	// calls. Construct one per goroutine (the Bind gives each per-path receive loop
285	// its own Codec and guards the shared send Codec with the Bind mutex).
286	type Codec struct {
287		obfKey     []byte
288		authKey    []byte
289		encScratch []byte // reused body buffer for Encode
290		decScratch []byte // reused body buffer for Decode
291	}
292	
293	// NewCodec derives the PSK-bound subkeys once and returns a reusable Codec. It
294	// fails only if the PSK is unset.
295	func NewCodec(psk config.Key) (*Codec, error) {
296		obfKey, authKey, err := subkeys(psk)
297		if err != nil {
298			return nil, err
299		}
300		return &Codec{obfKey: obfKey, authKey: authKey}, nil
301	}
302	
303	// Encode appends the wire encoding of f to dst and returns the extended slice,
304	// letting the caller reuse one buffer across sends (pass dst[:0]). It fails only
305	// if the system CSPRNG is unavailable.
306	func (c *Codec) Encode(dst []byte, f Frame) ([]byte, error) {
307		// Build the plaintext body (kind || header || payload) in reusable scratch.
308		c.encScratch = f.appendBody(c.encScratch[:0])
309		body := c.encScratch
310	
311		start := len(dst)
312		dst = append(dst, make([]byte, nonceLen)...)
313		nonce := dst[start : start+nonceLen]
314		if _, err := rand.Read(nonce); err != nil {
315			return nil, fmt.Errorf("frame: read nonce: %w", err)
316		}
317	
318		// Obfuscate the body in scratch, then append it; a single keystream init.
319		obfuscate(c.obfKey, nonce, body)
320		dst = append(dst, body...)
321	
322		if f.Kind().authenticated() {
323			// After the append above, nonce and obfBody are contiguous in dst's
324			// (possibly reallocated) backing array; tag over nonce||obfBody.
325			nonce = dst[start : start+nonceLen]
326			obfBody := dst[start+nonceLen:]
327			dst = append(dst, tag(c.authKey, nonce, obfBody)...)
328		}
329		return dst, nil
330	}
331	
332	// Decode parses a wire frame. It verifies the MAC of authenticated frames and
333	// rejects tampered or PSK-mismatched ones. It never panics on malformed input.
334	// The returned frame's payload is a fresh copy, so the caller may reuse raw and
335	// the Codec's scratch immediately.
336	//
337	// Authentication guarantee: a frame is accepted as an authenticated kind
338	// (CONTROL/PROBE) only if its MAC verifies under the PSK, so tampered or
339	// PSK-mismatched authenticated frames are rejected. Flipping the (obfuscated)
340	// kind byte can re-label an authenticated frame as an unauthenticated kind
341	// (DATA/PARITY), which then decodes without a MAC — this is not a downgrade
342	// break, because DATA/PARITY are forgeable by design (the inner WireGuard layer
343	// authenticates the real payload). No mutation can make a frame decode as an
344	// authentic CONTROL/PROBE.
345	func (c *Codec) Decode(raw []byte) (Frame, error) {
346		// Need at least the nonce plus one body byte (the kind discriminant).
347		if len(raw) < nonceLen+1 {
348			return nil, fmt.Errorf("%w: %d bytes, need >= %d", ErrMalformed, len(raw), nonceLen+1)
349		}
350	
351		nonce := raw[:nonceLen]
352		rest := raw[nonceLen:]
353	
354		// One keystream for the whole decode: init the cipher once, consume the
355		// first keystream byte to recover the (obfuscated) kind, then continue the
356		// SAME keystream over the remaining body. This avoids the old peekByte's
357		// second ChaCha20 init.
358		cipher, err := chacha20.NewUnauthenticatedCipher(c.obfKey, nonce)
359		if err != nil {
360			// obfKey is subkeyLen and nonce is nonceLen, so init cannot fail; a
361			// failure indicates a programmer error.
362			panic(fmt.Sprintf("frame: chacha20 init: %v", err))
363		}
364		kindByte := []byte{rest[0]}
365		cipher.XORKeyStream(kindByte, kindByte)
366		kind := Kind(kindByte[0])
367		if !kind.valid() {
368			return nil, fmt.Errorf("%w: unknown kind %d", ErrMalformed, uint8(kind))
369		}
370	
371		obfBody := rest
372		if kind.authenticated() {
373			if len(rest) < tagLen+1 {
374				return nil, fmt.Errorf("%w: authenticated frame too short", ErrMalformed)
375			}
376			obfBody = rest[:len(rest)-tagLen]
377			gotTag := rest[len(rest)-tagLen:]
378			wantTag := tag(c.authKey, nonce, obfBody)
379			if !hmac.Equal(gotTag, wantTag) {
380				return nil, ErrAuth
381			}
382		}
383	
384		// Reconstruct the plaintext body in reusable scratch. Byte 0 is the kind we
385		// already recovered; the remaining bytes continue the same keystream.
386		c.decScratch = append(c.decScratch[:0], obfBody...)
387		body := c.decScratch
388		body[0] = kindByte[0]
389		if len(body) > 1 {
390			cipher.XORKeyStream(body[1:], body[1:])
391		}
392		return decodeBody(kind, body[1:])
393	}
394	
395	// Encode serializes f into a self-contained wire frame under the given PSK. It
396	// is a convenience wrapper that builds a one-shot Codec; the per-datagram
397	// datapath uses a long-lived Codec instead (see Codec / defect D5). It fails
398	// only if the PSK is unset or the system CSPRNG is unavailable.
399	func Encode(psk config.Key, f Frame) ([]byte, error) {
400		c, err := NewCodec(psk)
401		if err != nil {
402			return nil, err
403		}
404		return c.Encode(nil, f)
405	}
406	
407	// Decode parses a wire frame under the given PSK. It is a convenience wrapper
408	// that builds a one-shot Codec; hot paths reuse a long-lived Codec instead.
409	func Decode(psk config.Key, raw []byte) (Frame, error) {
410		c, err := NewCodec(psk)
411		if err != nil {
412			return nil, err
413		}
414		return c.Decode(raw)
415	}
416	
417	// decodeBody parses the header+payload region (the body after the kind byte).
418	func decodeBody(kind Kind, b []byte) (Frame, error) {
419		r := reader{b: b}
420		switch kind {
421		case KindData:
422			seq, e1 := r.u64()
423			pathID, e2 := r.u8()
424			group, e3 := r.u32()
425			fecIndex, e4 := r.u8()
426			flags, e5 := r.u8()
427			if err := firstErr(e1, e2, e3, e4, e5); err != nil {
428				return nil, err
429			}
430			return Data{OuterSeq: seq, PathID: pathID, FECGroup: group, FECIndex: fecIndex, Flags: flags, Payload: r.rest()}, nil
431		case KindParity:
432			group, e1 := r.u32()
433			idx, e2 := r.u16()
434			dataCount, e3 := r.u8()
435			pathID, e4 := r.u8()
436			if err := firstErr(e1, e2, e3, e4); err != nil {
437				return nil, err
438			}
439			return Parity{FECGroup: group, ParityIndex: idx, DataCount: dataCount, PathID: pathID, Payload: r.rest()}, nil
440		case KindProbe:
441			pathID, e1 := r.u8()
442			probeSeq, e2 := r.u64()
443			ts, e3 := r.u64()
444			echo, e4 := r.u8()
445			sessionID, e5 := r.u64()
446			challenge, e6 := r.u64()
447			if err := firstErr(e1, e2, e3, e4, e5, e6); err != nil {
448				return nil, err
449			}
450			return Probe{PathID: pathID, ProbeSeq: probeSeq, TimestampNanos: int64(ts), IsEcho: echo != 0, SessionID: sessionID, Challenge: challenge, Payload: r.rest()}, nil
451		case KindControl:
452			ctype, e1 := r.u8()
453			seq, e2 := r.u64()
454			if err := firstErr(e1, e2); err != nil {
455				return nil, err
456			}
457			return Control{ControlType: ctype, Seq: seq, Payload: r.rest()}, nil
458		default:
459			// Unreachable: kind was validated by the caller.
460			return nil, fmt.Errorf("%w: unknown kind %d", ErrMalformed, uint8(kind))
461		}
462	}
463	
464	// subkeys derives the obfuscation and authentication subkeys from the PSK.
465	func subkeys(psk config.Key) (obfKey, authKey []byte, err error) {
466		if !psk.IsSet() {
467			return nil, nil, errors.New("frame: PSK is not set")
468		}
469		secret := psk.Bytes()
470		obfKey, err = hkdf.Key(sha256.New, secret[:], nil, infoObf, subkeyLen)
471		if err != nil {
472			return nil, nil, fmt.Errorf("frame: derive obf key: %w", err)
473		}
474		authKey, err = hkdf.Key(sha256.New, secret[:], nil, infoAuth, subkeyLen)
475		if err != nil {
476			return nil, nil, fmt.Errorf("frame: derive auth key: %w", err)
477		}
478		return obfKey, authKey, nil
479	}
480	
481	// obfuscate XORs body in place with the XChaCha20 keystream derived from obfKey
482	// and nonce. It is its own inverse.
483	func obfuscate(obfKey, nonce, body []byte) {
484		c, err := chacha20.NewUnauthenticatedCipher(obfKey, nonce)
485		if err != nil {
486			// obfKey is always subkeyLen and nonce is always nonceLen, so the cipher
487			// construction cannot fail; a failure indicates a programmer error.
488			panic(fmt.Sprintf("frame: chacha20 init: %v", err))
489		}
490		c.XORKeyStream(body, body)
491	}
492	
493	// tag computes the truncated Encrypt-then-MAC authentication tag over
494	// nonce||obfBody.
495	func tag(authKey, nonce, obfBody []byte) []byte {
496		m := hmac.New(sha256.New, authKey)
497		m.Write(nonce)
498		m.Write(obfBody)
499		return m.Sum(nil)[:tagLen]
500	}
501	
502	// reader is a fail-fast big-endian cursor over a byte slice.
503	type reader struct {
504		b   []byte
505		off int
506	}
507	
508	func (r *reader) need(n int) error {
509		if r.off+n > len(r.b) {
510			return fmt.Errorf("%w: need %d more bytes at offset %d", ErrMalformed, n, r.off)
511		}
512		return nil
513	}
514	
515	func (r *reader) u8() (uint8, error) {
516		if err := r.need(1); err != nil {
517			return 0, err
518		}
519		v := r.b[r.off]
520		r.off++
521		return v, nil
522	}
523	
524	func (r *reader) u16() (uint16, error) {
525		if err := r.need(2); err != nil {
526			return 0, err
527		}
528		v := binary.BigEndian.Uint16(r.b[r.off:])
529		r.off += 2
530		return v, nil
531	}
532	
533	func (r *reader) u32() (uint32, error) {
534		if err := r.need(4); err != nil {
535			return 0, err
536		}
537		v := binary.BigEndian.Uint32(r.b[r.off:])
538		r.off += 4
539		return v, nil
540	}
541	
542	func (r *reader) u64() (uint64, error) {
543		if err := r.need(8); err != nil {
544			return 0, err
545		}
546		v := binary.BigEndian.Uint64(r.b[r.off:])
547		r.off += 8
548		return v, nil
549	}
550	
551	// rest returns the remaining (opaque payload) bytes as a fresh copy so the
552	// decoded frame never aliases the caller's buffer.
553	func (r *reader) rest() []byte {
554		n := len(r.b) - r.off
555		if n == 0 {
556			r.off = len(r.b)
557			return nil
558		}
559		out := make([]byte, n)
560		copy(out, r.b[r.off:])
561		r.off = len(r.b)
562		return out
563	}
564	
565	func firstErr(errs ...error) error {
566		for _, e := range errs {
567			if e != nil {
568				return e
569			}
570		}
571		return nil
572	}
573

content.content:
1	package config
2	
3	import (
4		"encoding/base64"
5		"errors"
6		"fmt"
7		"math"
8		"net/netip"
9		"strconv"
10		"strings"
11		"time"
12	
13		awgdevice "github.com/amnezia-vpn/amneziawg-go/device"
14	)
15	
16	// Role selects which end of the tunnel this process runs as. It is an explicit,
17	// required field; the role is never inferred from other configuration.
18	type Role string
19	
20	const (
21		// RoleEdge is the mobile Linux box that bonds the WAN uplinks.
22		RoleEdge Role = "edge"
23		// RoleConcentrator is the public-IP VPS that terminates the tunnel.
24		RoleConcentrator Role = "concentrator"
25	)
26	
27	func (r Role) valid() bool {
28		return r == RoleEdge || r == RoleConcentrator
29	}
30	
31	// keyLen is the byte length of a Curve25519 WireGuard key and of a raw
32	// outer-control PSK, before base64 encoding.
33	const keyLen = 32
34	
35	// Config is the whole wanbond configuration, shared by both roles and parsed
36	// from a single TOML file.
37	type Config struct {
38		Role      Role            `toml:"role"`
39		Paths     []Path          `toml:"paths"`
40		WireGuard WireGuard       `toml:"wireguard"`
41		Amnezia   Amnezia         `toml:"amnezia"`
42		PSK       Key             `toml:"psk"`
43		Metrics   Metrics         `toml:"metrics"`
44		Log       Log             `toml:"log"`
45		Scheduler SchedulerConfig `toml:"scheduler"`
46		FEC       FEC             `toml:"fec"`
47	}
48	
49	// SchedulerPolicy selects the send-side path-selection policy the multipath Bind
50	// runs. It is a bounded enum: the P1 active-backup failover (default) or the T21
51	// weighted-aggregation policy. The policy is an explicit config choice — active-
52	// backup stays selectable so the P1 behaviour is never removed, only extended.
53	type SchedulerPolicy string
54	
55	const (
56		// PolicyActiveBackup is the P1 single-active-path failover scheduler: all egress
57		// on the highest-priority live path, every other path idle (data-thrift by
58		// construction). It is the default when [scheduler] is omitted.
59		PolicyActiveBackup SchedulerPolicy = "active-backup"
60		// PolicyWeighted is the T21 weighted-aggregation scheduler: under load a single
61		// flow is striped across both paths in proportion to per-path RTT/loss-derived
62		// weight, collapsing to the primary at low load (5G stays ~idle) with hysteresis,
63		// and per-path send-pacing to bound bufferbloat.
64		PolicyWeighted SchedulerPolicy = "weighted"
65	)
66	
67	func (p SchedulerPolicy) valid() bool {
68		return p == PolicyActiveBackup || p == PolicyWeighted
69	}
70	
71	// Weighted-aggregation policy defaults (T21). They are applied when [scheduler]
72	// selects the weighted policy but leaves a knob at its zero value, so a minimal
73	// `policy = "weighted"` block is usable without hand-tuning every threshold. They
74	// are conservative bring-up values; the empirical per-path pace is sized from a
75	// measured BDP in a bandwidth-capped fixture (P0 findings §7, deferred to T35/T23),
76	// so the shipped default leaves pacing DISABLED and only bounds distribution.
77	const (
78		// defaultPerPathCapacityFPS is the reference per-path capacity, in frame-selection
79		// slots per second, that the aggregation load-gate compares offered load against
80		// AND (when pacing is enabled) the per-path token-bucket refill rate. It is a
81		// synthetic proxy for path capacity: there is no measured BDP (P0 §7), so capacity
82		// is expressed in the only unit the scheduler sees — Pick() invocations per second.
83		defaultPerPathCapacityFPS = 10000.0
84		// defaultEngageFraction engages aggregation once offered load exceeds this
85		// fraction of one path's capacity — i.e. a single path is nearly saturated.
86		defaultEngageFraction = 0.9
87		// defaultDisengageFraction collapses back to primary-only once offered load falls
88		// below this fraction of one path's capacity. It is strictly below the engage
89		// fraction: the gap is the hysteresis band that stops the 5G path from dribbling
90		// on/off around a single threshold (requirement 2, data-thrift).
91		defaultDisengageFraction = 0.5
92		// defaultCollapseDwell is how long offered load must stay continuously below the
93		// disengage fraction before aggregation collapses to primary-only — the temporal
94		// half of the hysteresis, mirroring the active-backup failback dwell so a brief
95		// lull does not repeatedly park then re-engage the metered path.
96		defaultCollapseDwell = 2 * time.Second
97		// defaultLoadTau is the time constant of the exponentially-weighted offered-load
98		// rate estimator. It smooths the instantaneous Pick() arrival rate so the gate
99		// reacts to sustained load, not single-frame bursts.
100		defaultLoadTau = 200 * time.Millisecond
101		// defaultPacingBurstFrames is the per-path token-bucket burst (in frame slots)
102		// when pacing is enabled: the bucket admits up to this many frames instantaneously
103		// before the refill rate binds, absorbing normal jitter without inflating a queue.
104		defaultPacingBurstFrames = 64.0
105		// defaultWeightRTTFloor floors the RTT in the weight formula so a path reporting a
106		// near-zero RTT (cold estimator, no samples yet) cannot be handed unbounded weight.
107		defaultWeightRTTFloor = 1 * time.Millisecond
108		// defaultWeightLossFloor floors the loss term under the square root so a zero-loss
109		// path gets a large-but-finite weight and two zero-loss paths split by inverse-RTT
110		// alone rather than both diverging.
111		defaultWeightLossFloor = 1e-3
112	)
113	
114	// SchedulerConfig selects and tunes the send scheduler. When the [scheduler] block
115	// is omitted the policy defaults to active-backup and every weighted knob is
116	// ignored, so existing configs keep the P1 behaviour unchanged. The weighted knobs
117	// are validated (and defaulted) only when the weighted policy is selected.
118	type SchedulerConfig struct {
119		// Policy selects the scheduler; defaults to active-backup when empty.
120		Policy SchedulerPolicy `toml:"policy"`
121	
122		// PerPathCapacityFPS is the reference per-path capacity in frame-selection slots
123		// per second: the denominator the aggregation load-gate compares offered load
124		// against, and the per-path pacing refill rate when PacingEnabled. Must be > 0
125		// under the weighted policy.
126		PerPathCapacityFPS float64 `toml:"per_path_capacity_fps"`
127		// EngageFraction engages aggregation when offered load exceeds
128		// EngageFraction*PerPathCapacityFPS. Must be in (0,1].
129		EngageFraction float64 `toml:"engage_fraction"`
130		// DisengageFraction collapses aggregation to primary-only once offered load stays
131		// below DisengageFraction*PerPathCapacityFPS for CollapseDwell. Must be in
132		// [0,EngageFraction) — strictly below EngageFraction so the two form a hysteresis
133		// band.
134		DisengageFraction float64 `toml:"disengage_fraction"`
135		// CollapseDwell is the sustained-low-load dwell before collapsing to primary-only
136		// (hysteresis). Must be >= 0.
137		CollapseDwell time.Duration `toml:"collapse_dwell"`
138		// LoadTau is the offered-load rate estimator's time constant. Must be > 0.
139		LoadTau time.Duration `toml:"load_tau"`

141		// PacingEnabled turns per-path send-pacing on. When false the token buckets are
142		// bypassed (a documented no-op — P0 §7 could not empirically size the pace in the
143		// unmetered fixture), but PerPathCapacityFPS still drives the aggregation gate.
144		PacingEnabled bool `toml:"pacing_enabled"`
145		// PacingBurstFrames is the per-path token-bucket burst in frame slots. Must be > 0
146		// when PacingEnabled.
147		PacingBurstFrames float64 `toml:"pacing_burst_frames"`
148	
149		// WeightRTTFloor floors RTT in the weight formula (must be > 0 under weighted).
150		WeightRTTFloor time.Duration `toml:"weight_rtt_floor"`
151		// WeightLossFloor floors the loss term under the square root (must be > 0 under
152		// weighted).
153		WeightLossFloor float64 `toml:"weight_loss_floor"`

156	// BDPSizing is a per-path pacing sizing derived from a MEASURED path, in the
157	// frame-selection-slot units the weighted scheduler pace uses (defect D22). It is the
158	// empirical alternative to the synthetic frame-count defaults: CapacityFPS replaces
159	// PerPathCapacityFPS and BurstFrames replaces PacingBurstFrames.
160	type BDPSizing struct {
161		// CapacityFPS is the sustained per-path frame rate the measured bottleneck bandwidth
162		// supports (the token-bucket refill rate and aggregation-gate denominator).
163		CapacityFPS float64
164		// BurstFrames is the bandwidth-delay product expressed in frames (bandwidth x RTT,
165		// i.e. one RTT of in-flight frames): the token-bucket burst that absorbs one RTT of
166		// jitter without building a standing queue.
167		BurstFrames float64
168	}
169	
170	// defaultAvgWireFrameBytes is the conservative average on-wire outer-frame size used
171	// when deriving the per-path pace from an operator-declared bandwidth at config load
172	// (T53): a full IPv4 path MTU, since a full-MTU DATA datagram occupies about one path
173	// MTU on the wire. It mirrors bind.DefaultPathMTU (1500) rather than importing it —
174	// internal/bind imports internal/config, so config cannot import bind without a cycle
175	// (the same mirror-with-cross-reference pattern as maxFECDeadline above). Sizing
176	// capacity with the full-MTU frame is the conservative floor: smaller average frames
177	// would yield a HIGHER frame rate, so this never over-paces a path.
178	const defaultAvgWireFrameBytes = 1500.0
179	
180	// SizePacingFromBDP derives the weighted scheduler's per-path pacing parameters from a
181	// measured path instead of the synthetic frame-count default (defect D22). The shipped
182	// defaultPerPathCapacityFPS (10000, ~115 Mbit/s at full MTU) sits far above a realistic
183	// slow uplink, so the aggregation gate may never engage and — with pacing enabled — the
184	// pace never binds; deriving capacity from the measured bottleneck bandwidth fixes both.
185	//
186	// capacity_fps = bandwidth / (8 * avg wire frame bytes) — frames/s the link sustains.
187	// burst_frames = capacity_fps * RTT — one RTT of in-flight frames (equivalently
188	// BDP_bytes / avg wire frame bytes, since the bandwidth-delay product BDP = bandwidth * RTT).
189	//
190	// bandwidthBitsPerSec is the measured bottleneck bandwidth (bits/s), rtt the measured
191	// path RTT, and avgWireFrameBytes the average on-wire outer-frame size (a full-MTU
192	// datagram plus frame.DataOverhead is the conservative choice). It fails fast on a
193	// non-positive input rather than emitting a nonsensical (zero/negative/Inf) sizing.
194	func SizePacingFromBDP(bandwidthBitsPerSec float64, rtt time.Duration, avgWireFrameBytes float64) (BDPSizing, error) {
195		if bandwidthBitsPerSec <= 0 {
196			return BDPSizing{}, fmt.Errorf("config: BDP sizing bandwidth must be > 0 bit/s, got %g", bandwidthBitsPerSec)
197		}
198		if rtt <= 0 {
199			return BDPSizing{}, fmt.Errorf("config: BDP sizing RTT must be > 0, got %s", rtt)
200		}
201		if avgWireFrameBytes <= 0 {
202			return BDPSizing{}, fmt.Errorf("config: BDP sizing average wire frame size must be > 0 bytes, got %g", avgWireFrameBytes)
203		}
204		const bitsPerByte = 8.0
205		capacityFPS := bandwidthBitsPerSec / (bitsPerByte * avgWireFrameBytes)
206		burstFrames := capacityFPS * rtt.Seconds()
207		return BDPSizing{CapacityFPS: capacityFPS, BurstFrames: burstFrames}, nil
208	}

210	// bandwidthUnit is a recognised link_bandwidth suffix and its bit/s multiplier.
211	type bandwidthUnit struct {
212		suffix string
213		mult   float64
214	}
215	
216	// bandwidthUnits are the accepted operator-facing bandwidth suffixes, longest-first so
217	// "gbit" is matched before "bit". SI decimal multipliers (k/M/G = 1e3/1e6/1e9) over a
218	// bit/s base; "bps" is accepted as an alias of "bit" (and "kbps"/"mbps"/"gbps" likewise).
219	var bandwidthUnits = []bandwidthUnit{
220		{"gbit", 1e9}, {"gbps", 1e9},
221		{"mbit", 1e6}, {"mbps", 1e6},
222		{"kbit", 1e3}, {"kbps", 1e3},
223		{"bit", 1}, {"bps", 1},
224	}
225	
226	// parseBandwidth parses an operator-declared link bandwidth such as "50Mbit", "1Gbit",
227	// or "500kbit" into bits per second. It requires an explicit bit/s unit suffix so a
228	// bare unitless number cannot be silently misread, and fails fast on an empty or
229	// otherwise unparseable value — a mistyped bandwidth is rejected at config load.
230	func parseBandwidth(s string) (float64, error) {
231		lower := strings.ToLower(strings.TrimSpace(s))
232		if lower == "" {
233			return 0, errors.New("empty bandwidth")
234		}
235		for _, u := range bandwidthUnits {
236			if !strings.HasSuffix(lower, u.suffix) {
237				continue
238			}
239			num := strings.TrimSpace(lower[:len(lower)-len(u.suffix)])
240			val, err := strconv.ParseFloat(num, 64)
241			if err != nil {
242				return 0, fmt.Errorf("invalid number %q", num)
243			}
244			return val * u.mult, nil
245		}
246		return 0, fmt.Errorf("missing bit/s unit suffix (want e.g. %q)", "50Mbit")
247	}
248	
249	// FEC group-close deadline default (T24). Applied only when [fec] is enabled but
250	// leaves the deadline at its zero value, so a minimal `enabled = true` block emits
251	// parity for a partially-filled group promptly under low load rather than stranding
252	// it until the size threshold fills.
253	const defaultFECDeadline = 5 * time.Millisecond
254	
255	// defaultAdaptiveSafetyFactor mirrors adaptivefec.DefaultSafetyFactor (1.5), the
256	// simulation-proven controller default, restated here (with this cross-reference rather
257	// than an import) so a minimal `adaptive = true` block gets the proven tuning without
258	// coupling config to the controller package. NOTE: 1.5 sizes M for the mean loss with
259	// modest variance headroom; a tight residual SLA under a specific loss/geometry may need a
260	// higher factor (e.g. at 5% loss with K=10 the 1.5 default sizes M=1, ~1% residual — raise
261	// this to lift M and drive the residual down).
262	const defaultAdaptiveSafetyFactor = 1.5
263	
264	// maxFECDeadline bounds the FEC group-close deadline at load time (T24, defect #4). It
265	// MUST stay at or below the multipath Bind's authoritative bound (bind.maxFECDeadline =
266	// resequencerTimeout/2 = 125ms): a group flushed by the deadline emits its parity
267	// `deadline` after opening, and the reconstructed frames must reach the receive
268	// resequencer BEFORE it skips the gap (its 250ms per-gap timeout) — otherwise recovery
269	// is structurally too late (the gap is skipped, the recovered frame dropped as past the
270	// release point) while /metrics would still count it reconstructed. Rejecting an
271	// over-large deadline at load makes that coupling fail-fast and explicit rather than
272	// silently defeating FEC. Kept in lockstep with bind.maxFECDeadline (the packages
273	// cannot import each other, so the value is mirrored with this cross-reference).
274	const maxFECDeadline = 125 * time.Millisecond
275	
276	// maxFECShards mirrors the Reed-Solomon field limit enforced in internal/fec: a
277	// coding group carries at most 256 shards (data + parity) total over GF(2^8). It
278	// is restated here so config load fails fast on an over-large ratio at the right
279	// locus, with the same bound the codec enforces internally.
280	const maxFECShards = 256
281	
282	// FEC configures the fixed-ratio Reed-Solomon forward-error-correction plane (T24).
283	// It is DISABLED by default (like pacing), so an existing config with no [fec] block
284	// runs the datapath exactly as before — FEC transparent, no parity on the wire. When
285	// enabled, each group of DataShards (K) inner datagrams is protected by ParityShards
286	// (M) parity frames, letting the receiver reconstruct up to M lost data frames per
287	// group at a fixed M/K parity overhead.
288	type FEC struct {
289		// Enabled turns the FEC plane on. When false every other field is ignored and the
290		// datapath carries no parity.
291		Enabled bool `toml:"enabled"`
292		// DataShards is K: the number of inner datagrams grouped before parity is emitted.
293		// Must be >= 1 when enabled.
294		DataShards int `toml:"data_shards"`
295		// ParityShards is M: the parity frames emitted per group and the maximum number of
296		// per-group data losses the receiver can recover. Must be >= 1 when enabled. In
297		// adaptive mode it is the CEILING (and the receiver's decoder cardinality) — the
298		// controller drives the per-group parity in [0,ParityShards] to track measured loss.
299		ParityShards int `toml:"parity_shards"`
300		// Deadline bounds grouping latency: a partially-filled group is flushed (parity
301		// emitted over its current data frames) once this much time has elapsed since its
302		// first frame. Defaults to defaultFECDeadline when enabled and left zero; must be
303		// > 0 after defaulting.
304		Deadline time.Duration `toml:"deadline"`
305		// Adaptive opts the send-side FEC into the closed-loop controller (T27/T29): the
306		// per-group parity count tracks the measured per-path loss instead of standing at
307		// the fixed ParityShards ratio, so a clean path spends near-zero overhead while a
308		// lossy one is masked. It is OFF by default, so an existing [fec] block keeps the
309		// fixed-ratio behaviour (T24) byte-for-byte. When true, ParityShards is reinterpreted
310		// as the controller's parity ceiling (see above); the receiver is unchanged because a
311		// group coded with fewer parity shards decodes against the ParityShards-ceiling codec
312		// unchanged (klauspost's parity is prefix-consistent).
313		Adaptive bool `toml:"adaptive"`
314		// SafetyFactor is the adaptive controller's headroom multiplier over the measured mean
315		// loss the per-group parity is sized to mask (adaptivefec.Config.SafetyFactor). It is
316		// the residual-loss LEVER: the controller sizes M so M/(K+M) >= SafetyFactor*loss, so a
317		// higher factor spends more parity to keep the post-recovery residual under a tighter
318		// bound against binomial per-group variance. Applies only in adaptive mode; defaults to
319		// defaultAdaptiveSafetyFactor (the simulation-proven controller default) when left zero
320		// AND target_residual is unset, and must be >= 1. Ignored (and must stay zero) in fixed
321		// mode, and mutually exclusive with target_residual (set one, not both).
322		SafetyFactor float64 `toml:"safety_factor"`
323		// TargetResidual is the adaptive controller's target POST-RECOVERY residual-loss SLA
324		// (a fraction in (0,1)) and the PRIMARY adaptive sizing surface (D26/T46): the
325		// controller derives the per-group parity M by inverting the binomial residual model
326		// E[max(0,D-M)]/K (D ~ Bin(K, smoothed loss)) to the smallest M meeting this target,
327		// capped at the parity ceiling (parity_shards). It SUPERSEDES safety_factor — set
328		// EITHER a residual SLA (target_residual, recommended: it maps an operator's loss
329		// budget directly to redundancy) OR the bare headroom multiplier (safety_factor), never
330		// both. Applies only in adaptive mode; unset (0) selects the safety_factor path. Must
331		// be in (0,1) when set. Ignored (and must stay zero) in fixed mode.
332		TargetResidual float64 `toml:"target_residual"`
333	}
334	
335	// applyDefaults fills the group-close deadline when FEC is enabled and the deadline
336	// was left at zero. It is a no-op for a disabled block, so a config that never turns
337	// FEC on keeps an empty [fec] surface.
338	func (f *FEC) applyDefaults() {
339		if !f.Enabled {
340			return
341		}
342		if f.Deadline == 0 {
343			f.Deadline = defaultFECDeadline
344		}
345		// The SafetyFactor default is applied only in the legacy sizing mode: when
346		// target_residual is set it is the primary surface and safety_factor stays inert
347		// (0), so defaulting it would spuriously trip the mutual-exclusion check.
348		if f.Adaptive && f.TargetResidual == 0 && f.SafetyFactor == 0 {
349			f.SafetyFactor = defaultAdaptiveSafetyFactor
350		}
351	}
352	
353	// validate enforces the FEC ratio invariants, failing fast so a mis-tuned parity
354	// ratio is rejected at load rather than panicking the codec at runtime. A disabled
355	// block needs no tuning. The bounds mirror internal/fec's own Config.validate.
356	func (f FEC) validate() error {
357		if !f.Enabled {
358			if f.Adaptive {
359				return errors.New("fec.adaptive = true requires fec.enabled = true (adaptive FEC is meaningless with the plane off)")
360			}
361			return nil
362		}
363		if f.DataShards < 1 {
364			return fmt.Errorf("fec.data_shards must be >= 1 when FEC is enabled, got %d", f.DataShards)
365		}
366		if f.ParityShards < 1 {
367			return fmt.Errorf("fec.parity_shards must be >= 1 when FEC is enabled, got %d", f.ParityShards)
368		}
369		if f.DataShards+f.ParityShards > maxFECShards {
370			return fmt.Errorf("fec.data_shards + fec.parity_shards must be <= %d (Reed-Solomon field limit), got %d", maxFECShards, f.DataShards+f.ParityShards)
371		}
372		if f.Deadline <= 0 {
373			return fmt.Errorf("fec.deadline must be > 0 when FEC is enabled, got %s", f.Deadline)
374		}
375		if f.Deadline > maxFECDeadline {
376			return fmt.Errorf("fec.deadline must be <= %s (safely below the receive resequencer's per-gap timeout so deadline-flushed recovery lands before the gap is skipped), got %s", maxFECDeadline, f.Deadline)
377		}
378		if f.Adaptive {
379			if f.TargetResidual != 0 {
380				// Residual-SLA sizing mode (primary): target_residual governs and safety_factor
381				// stays inert, so setting both is an ambiguous mis-config — reject it. NaN is
382				// caught explicitly because every ordered comparison against it is false.
383				if math.IsNaN(f.TargetResidual) || f.TargetResidual <= 0 || f.TargetResidual >= 1 {
384					return fmt.Errorf("fec.target_residual must be a finite value in (0,1), got %g", f.TargetResidual)
385				}
386				if f.SafetyFactor != 0 {
387					return fmt.Errorf("fec.safety_factor and fec.target_residual are mutually exclusive; target_residual is the primary residual-SLA surface — set one, not both")
388				}
389			} else if f.SafetyFactor < 1 {
390				// SafetyFactor is defaulted to defaultAdaptiveSafetyFactor when zero, so a value
391				// below 1 here is an explicit mis-set: the controller requires >= 1 (masking less
392				// than the mean loss is nonsensical), matching adaptivefec.Config.Validate.
393				return fmt.Errorf("fec.safety_factor must be >= 1 in adaptive mode, got %g", f.SafetyFactor)
394			}
395		} else if f.SafetyFactor != 0 || f.TargetResidual != 0 {
396			return fmt.Errorf("fec.safety_factor / fec.target_residual are only meaningful in adaptive mode (set fec.adaptive = true), got safety_factor=%g target_residual=%g", f.SafetyFactor, f.TargetResidual)
397		}
398		return nil
399	}

401	// Path is one physical WAN uplink. The edge binds each path's UDP socket to
402	// SourceAddr so the upstream router pins it to the intended WAN; the
403	// concentrator learns the real per-path endpoints from authenticated traffic.
404	type Path struct {
405		// Name is a stable human-readable identifier for the path (e.g. "starlink").
406		Name string `toml:"name"`
407		// SourceAddr is the local source IP the path's socket binds to.
408		SourceAddr netip.Addr `toml:"-"`
409		// SourceAddrRaw is the TOML string form of SourceAddr; parsed in normalize.
410		SourceAddrRaw string `toml:"source_addr"`
411		// DestAddr is the OPTIONAL per-path concentrator endpoint ("ip:port") the
412		// edge sends this path's datagrams to. It is unset on the concentrator (which
413		// learns edge endpoints from traffic) and optional on the edge: when a single
414		// public concentrator IP fronts all uplinks (the common source-routed case)
415		// the peer's wireguard endpoint is reused for every path; when the paths reach
416		// the concentrator on distinct addresses, set dest_addr per path.
417		DestAddr netip.AddrPort `toml:"-"`
418		// DestAddrRaw is the TOML string form of DestAddr; parsed in normalize.
419		DestAddrRaw string `toml:"dest_addr"`
420		// LinkBandwidthBitsPerSec is the OPERATOR-DECLARED bottleneck bandwidth of this
421		// uplink in bits/s, parsed from LinkBandwidthRaw in normalize. It is used ONLY to
422		// size the weighted scheduler's per-path pace from the bandwidth-delay product
423		// (SizePacingFromBDP) when the weighted policy runs with pacing ENABLED (T53, Q20);
424		// it is OPERATOR-DECLARED, not runtime-measured — wanbond never auto-tunes it live.
425		// Zero means "not declared" (the synthetic default pace is kept).
426		LinkBandwidthBitsPerSec float64 `toml:"-"`
427		// LinkBandwidthRaw is the TOML string form of the declared bandwidth, e.g.
428		// "50Mbit" / "1Gbit" / "500kbit" (SI bit/s units; the "bit" suffix may be written
429		// "bps"). Parsed in normalize; a non-positive or unparseable value fails fast.
430		LinkBandwidthRaw string `toml:"link_bandwidth"`
431		// LinkRTT is the OPERATOR-DECLARED baseline RTT of this uplink, parsed from
432		// LinkRTTRaw in normalize. It is the delay term of the bandwidth-delay-product pace
433		// burst (one RTT of in-flight frames); required (> 0) when LinkBandwidth is set and
434		// pacing is enabled under the weighted policy, ignored otherwise.
435		LinkRTT time.Duration `toml:"-"`
436		// LinkRTTRaw is the TOML Go-duration string form of LinkRTT, e.g. "45ms". Parsed in
437		// normalize; an unparseable or non-positive value fails fast.
438		LinkRTTRaw string `toml:"link_rtt"`
439	}

441	// WireGuard holds the inner tunnel's key material.
442	type WireGuard struct {
443		PrivateKey Key    `toml:"private_key"`
444		Peers      []Peer `toml:"peers"`
445		// ListenPort is the UDP port the concentrator listens on; 0 on the edge.
446		ListenPort uint16 `toml:"listen_port"`
447	}

449	// Peer is one WireGuard peer.
450	type Peer struct {
451		PublicKey Key `toml:"public_key"`
452		// Endpoint is the peer's SINGLE tunnel address (edge -> concentrator); the
453		// legacy single-endpoint config form, mutually exclusive with EndpointsRaw.
454		// Empty on the concentrator, which roams the edge's endpoint dynamically.
455		// A bare `endpoint = "..."` is normalized in resolveEndpoints to a one-
456		// element Endpoints list, so it stays behavior-identical to the pre-T54
457		// config surface.
458		Endpoint string `toml:"endpoint"`
459		// EndpointsRaw is the ORDERED list of concentrator (peer) endpoint address:port
460		// strings for the edge-side hub-failover config surface (Q18): index 0 is the
461		// active/primary concentrator, the rest are ordered standbys. Edge-only; a
462		// concentrator declaring it is a config error (validate). Mutually exclusive
463		// with the legacy Endpoint field.
464		EndpointsRaw []string `toml:"endpoints"`
465		// Endpoints is EndpointsRaw (or the single legacy Endpoint, as a one-element
466		// list) parsed to netip.AddrPort and de-duplicated; populated by
467		// resolveEndpoints in normalize(). This is the shape T57's hub-failover
468		// switch consumes: Endpoints[0] is the active concentrator, Endpoints[1:]
469		// are the ordered standbys to fail over to in order when all paths to the
470		// active one are down.
471		Endpoints []netip.AddrPort `toml:"-"`
472		// AllowedIPs are the CIDR ranges routed to this peer.
473		AllowedIPs []string `toml:"allowed_ips"`
474	}
475	
476	// resolveEndpoints canonicalizes the peer's endpoint config into the ordered
477	// Endpoints list (Q18): the legacy single Endpoint field and the new ordered
478	// EndpointsRaw list are mutually exclusive input forms, but a bare `endpoint =
479	// "..."` is normalized here to a ONE-ELEMENT Endpoints list — so it stays
480	// behavior-identical to the pre-T54 single-defaultRemote config surface. Each
481	// entry must parse as netip.AddrPort ("host:port"), the same format
482	// bind.Multipath.ParseEndpoint requires of the UAPI endpoint string at
483	// runtime, and duplicates within the list are rejected.
484	func (p *Peer) resolveEndpoints() error {
485		if p.Endpoint != "" && len(p.EndpointsRaw) > 0 {
486			return errors.New("endpoint and endpoints are mutually exclusive; endpoint is the single-entry legacy form of endpoints")
487		}
488		raw := p.EndpointsRaw
489		if p.Endpoint != "" {
490			raw = []string{p.Endpoint}
491		}
492		seen := make(map[netip.AddrPort]struct{}, len(raw))
493		endpoints := make([]netip.AddrPort, 0, len(raw))
494		for _, s := range raw {
495			ap, err := netip.ParseAddrPort(s)
496			if err != nil {
497				return fmt.Errorf("invalid endpoint %q: %w", s, err)
498			}
499			if _, dup := seen[ap]; dup {
500				return fmt.Errorf("duplicate endpoint %q", s)
501			}
502			seen[ap] = struct{}{}
503			endpoints = append(endpoints, ap)
504		}
505		p.Endpoints = endpoints
506		return nil
507	}

509	// Amnezia holds the amneziawg-go obfuscation parameters. They must match on both
510	// ends for the handshake to succeed; they are defense-in-depth only.
511	//
512	// The block is "all-or-nothing": either every junk/size knob is left zero (plain
513	// WireGuard) or the whole obfuscation set (jc, jmin, jmax, s1, s2) is specified.
514	// A PARTIAL block silently produces an obfuscation profile the two ends cannot
515	// agree on, so validate rejects it at load (defect D1). The four magic headers
516	// (h1-h4) default to the standard message-type values 1..4 when omitted.
517	type Amnezia struct {
518		Jc   int    `toml:"jc"`
519		Jmin int    `toml:"jmin"`
520		Jmax int    `toml:"jmax"`
521		S1   int    `toml:"s1"`
522		S2   int    `toml:"s2"`
523		H1   uint32 `toml:"h1"`
524		H2   uint32 `toml:"h2"`
525		H3   uint32 `toml:"h3"`
526		H4   uint32 `toml:"h4"`
527	}
528	
529	// defaultMagicHeaders are the standard WireGuard message-type headers: initiation,
530	// response, cookie-reply, and transport. amneziawg-go treats any magic header <= 4
531	// as "use the standard type", so 1..4 is the canonical "headers not obfuscated"
532	// profile. wanbond emits them explicitly (rather than 0) so a configured amnezia
533	// block always carries a complete, self-consistent set of magic headers.
534	var defaultMagicHeaders = [4]uint32{1, 2, 3, 4}
535	
536	// Configured reports whether the amnezia block carries any obfuscation parameter.
537	// An all-zero block leaves the engine in plain WireGuard mode.
538	func (a Amnezia) Configured() bool {
539		return a.Jc != 0 || a.Jmin != 0 || a.Jmax != 0 || a.S1 != 0 || a.S2 != 0 ||
540			a.H1 != 0 || a.H2 != 0 || a.H3 != 0 || a.H4 != 0
541	}
542	
543	// applyDefaults fills in the standard magic headers (1..4) when the block is
544	// configured but no header was given, so the UAPI renderer emits an explicit,
545	// complete header set instead of h1=0..h4=0. It is a no-op for an unconfigured
546	// block and for one that already sets any header (a partial header set is left
547	// intact so validate can reject it).
548	func (a *Amnezia) applyDefaults() {
549		if !a.Configured() {
550			return
551		}
552		if a.H1 == 0 && a.H2 == 0 && a.H3 == 0 && a.H4 == 0 {
553			a.H1, a.H2, a.H3, a.H4 = defaultMagicHeaders[0], defaultMagicHeaders[1], defaultMagicHeaders[2], defaultMagicHeaders[3]
554		}
555	}

557	// Metrics configures the localhost Prometheus endpoint.
558	type Metrics struct {
559		// Listen is the address the /metrics endpoint binds to; must be loopback.
560		Listen string `toml:"listen"`

568	// Key is a 32-byte Curve25519 key or PSK, carried in TOML as standard base64.
569	type Key struct {
570		bytes [keyLen]byte
571		set   bool
572	}
573	
574	// UnmarshalText decodes a base64 key. An empty string leaves the Key unset so
575	// optional keys can be distinguished from present-but-invalid ones.
576	func (k *Key) UnmarshalText(text []byte) error {
577		if len(text) == 0 {
578			return nil
579		}
580		raw, err := base64.StdEncoding.DecodeString(string(text))
581		if err != nil {
582			return fmt.Errorf("key is not valid base64: %w", err)
583		}
584		if len(raw) != keyLen {
585			return fmt.Errorf("key must decode to %d bytes, got %d", keyLen, len(raw))
586		}
587		copy(k.bytes[:], raw)
588		k.set = true
589		return nil
590	}
591	
592	// IsSet reports whether the key was present and valid.
593	func (k Key) IsSet() bool { return k.set }
594	
595	// Bytes returns the raw key material.
596	func (k Key) Bytes() [keyLen]byte { return k.bytes }

598	// normalize parses the string-typed fields (addresses) into their typed forms.
599	func (c *Config) normalize() error {
600		for i := range c.Paths {
601			p := &c.Paths[i]
602			if p.SourceAddrRaw != "" {
603				addr, err := netip.ParseAddr(p.SourceAddrRaw)
604				if err != nil {
605					return fmt.Errorf("path %q: invalid source_addr %q: %w", p.Name, p.SourceAddrRaw, err)
606				}
607				p.SourceAddr = addr
608			}
609			if p.DestAddrRaw != "" {
610				dst, err := netip.ParseAddrPort(p.DestAddrRaw)
611				if err != nil {
612					return fmt.Errorf("path %q: invalid dest_addr %q: %w", p.Name, p.DestAddrRaw, err)
613				}
614				p.DestAddr = dst
615			}
616			if p.LinkBandwidthRaw != "" {
617				bw, err := parseBandwidth(p.LinkBandwidthRaw)
618				if err != nil {
619					return fmt.Errorf("path %q: invalid link_bandwidth %q: %w", p.Name, p.LinkBandwidthRaw, err)
620				}
621				if bw <= 0 {
622					return fmt.Errorf("path %q: link_bandwidth must be > 0, got %q", p.Name, p.LinkBandwidthRaw)
623				}
624				p.LinkBandwidthBitsPerSec = bw
625			}
626			if p.LinkRTTRaw != "" {
627				rtt, err := time.ParseDuration(p.LinkRTTRaw)
628				if err != nil {
629					return fmt.Errorf("path %q: invalid link_rtt %q: %w", p.Name, p.LinkRTTRaw, err)
630				}
631				if rtt <= 0 {
632					return fmt.Errorf("path %q: link_rtt must be > 0, got %q", p.Name, p.LinkRTTRaw)
633				}
634				p.LinkRTT = rtt
635			}
636		}
637		for i := range c.WireGuard.Peers {
638			if err := c.WireGuard.Peers[i].resolveEndpoints(); err != nil {
639				return fmt.Errorf("wireguard peer %d: %w", i, err)
640			}
641		}
642		c.Amnezia.applyDefaults()
643		// Derive the weighted pace from any operator-declared per-link bandwidth BEFORE
644		// applyDefaults, so it can (a) see the raw zero PerPathCapacityFPS to distinguish an
645		// explicit knob from the default and (b) set the derived capacity/burst that
646		// applyDefaults then leaves intact (it only fills a knob left at zero). Under a
647		// disabled or non-weighted pacing it is a no-op, so the synthetic default is kept.
648		if err := c.deriveWeightedPacingFromBDP(); err != nil {
649			return err
650		}
651		c.Scheduler.applyDefaults()
652		c.FEC.applyDefaults()
653		return nil
654	}
655	
656	// deriveWeightedPacingFromBDP sizes the weighted scheduler's per-path pace from the
657	// operator-declared per-link bandwidth (T53, Q20) via SizePacingFromBDP instead of the
658	// synthetic defaultPerPathCapacityFPS. It runs only under the weighted policy with
659	// pacing ENABLED and at least one declared link_bandwidth; otherwise a declared
660	// bandwidth is inert (pacing ships DISABLED by default), so an unrelated config is
661	// untouched. The value is OPERATOR-DECLARED and fixed at load — NOT runtime auto-tuning
662	// (Q20 rejected a live control loop for the pilot).
663	//
664	// The scheduler carries a SINGLE reference per-path capacity applied to every path's
665	// token bucket, so a heterogeneous link set is sized to the BOTTLENECK: the slowest
666	// declared link governs the shared pace, because pacing any path faster than the
667	// slowest link's capacity would let that link build the very standing queue pacing
668	// exists to prevent. All paths must therefore declare a bandwidth (all-or-nothing) so
669	// the bottleneck is well-defined across the whole path set.
670	func (c *Config) deriveWeightedPacingFromBDP() error {
671		s := &c.Scheduler
672		if s.Policy != PolicyWeighted || !s.PacingEnabled {
673			return nil
674		}
675		declared := 0
676		for i := range c.Paths {
677			if c.Paths[i].LinkBandwidthBitsPerSec > 0 {
678				declared++
679			}
680		}
681		if declared == 0 {
682			return nil // no declared bandwidth: the synthetic default pace is preserved.
683		}
684		if declared != len(c.Paths) {
685			return fmt.Errorf("scheduler pacing: link_bandwidth must be declared on ALL paths or none (got %d of %d) — the shared per-path pace is sized to the slowest declared link, which is undefined with a partial declaration", declared, len(c.Paths))
686		}
687		if s.PerPathCapacityFPS != 0 || s.PacingBurstFrames != 0 {
688			return errors.New("scheduler.per_path_capacity_fps / pacing_burst_frames and per-path link_bandwidth are mutually exclusive: declare the link bandwidth (BDP-derived pace) OR set the raw frame-slot knobs, not both")
689		}
690		var bottleneck BDPSizing
691		for i := range c.Paths {
692			p := &c.Paths[i]
693			if p.LinkRTT <= 0 {
694				return fmt.Errorf("path %q: link_rtt is required (> 0) when link_bandwidth is set under weighted pacing — it is the delay term of the bandwidth-delay-product burst", p.Name)
695			}
696			sz, err := SizePacingFromBDP(p.LinkBandwidthBitsPerSec, p.LinkRTT, defaultAvgWireFrameBytes)
697			if err != nil {
698				return fmt.Errorf("path %q: %w", p.Name, err)
699			}
700			if i == 0 || sz.CapacityFPS < bottleneck.CapacityFPS {
701				bottleneck = sz
702			}
703		}
704		s.PerPathCapacityFPS = bottleneck.CapacityFPS
705		s.PacingBurstFrames = bottleneck.BurstFrames
706		return nil
707	}
708	
709	// applyDefaults selects active-backup when no policy is given and, only under the
710	// weighted policy, fills any weighted knob left at its zero value with its default.
711	// It is a no-op for the active-backup policy: those weighted knobs are inert there,
712	// so leaving them zero keeps the config surface for a P1 deployment empty.
713	func (s *SchedulerConfig) applyDefaults() {
714		if s.Policy == "" {
715			s.Policy = PolicyActiveBackup
716		}
717		if s.Policy != PolicyWeighted {
718			return
719		}
720		if s.PerPathCapacityFPS == 0 {
721			s.PerPathCapacityFPS = defaultPerPathCapacityFPS
722		}
723		if s.EngageFraction == 0 {
724			s.EngageFraction = defaultEngageFraction
725		}
726		if s.DisengageFraction == 0 {
727			s.DisengageFraction = defaultDisengageFraction
728		}
729		if s.CollapseDwell == 0 {
730			s.CollapseDwell = defaultCollapseDwell
731		}
732		if s.LoadTau == 0 {
733			s.LoadTau = defaultLoadTau
734		}
735		if s.PacingBurstFrames == 0 {
736			s.PacingBurstFrames = defaultPacingBurstFrames
737		}
738		if s.WeightRTTFloor == 0 {
739			s.WeightRTTFloor = defaultWeightRTTFloor
740		}
741		if s.WeightLossFloor == 0 {
742			s.WeightLossFloor = defaultWeightLossFloor
743		}
744	}
745	
746	// validate enforces the scheduler policy invariants. active-backup needs no tuning.
747	// The weighted policy fails fast on any out-of-range knob so a mis-tuned
748	// aggregation policy is rejected at load rather than misbehaving at runtime (the
749	// hysteresis band, in particular, is only a band when disengage < engage).
750	func (s SchedulerConfig) validate() error {
751		if !s.Policy.valid() {
752			return fmt.Errorf("scheduler.policy must be %q or %q, got %q", PolicyActiveBackup, PolicyWeighted, s.Policy)
753		}
754		if s.Policy != PolicyWeighted {
755			return nil
756		}
757		if s.PerPathCapacityFPS <= 0 {
758			return fmt.Errorf("scheduler.per_path_capacity_fps must be > 0 under the weighted policy, got %g", s.PerPathCapacityFPS)
759		}
760		if s.EngageFraction <= 0 || s.EngageFraction > 1 {
761			return fmt.Errorf("scheduler.engage_fraction must be in (0,1], got %g", s.EngageFraction)
762		}
763		if s.DisengageFraction < 0 || s.DisengageFraction >= s.EngageFraction {
764			return fmt.Errorf("scheduler.disengage_fraction must be in [0,engage_fraction=%g) to form a hysteresis band, got %g", s.EngageFraction, s.DisengageFraction)
765		}
766		if s.CollapseDwell < 0 {
767			return fmt.Errorf("scheduler.collapse_dwell must be >= 0, got %s", s.CollapseDwell)
768		}
769		if s.LoadTau <= 0 {
770			return fmt.Errorf("scheduler.load_tau must be > 0, got %s", s.LoadTau)
771		}
772		if s.PacingEnabled && s.PacingBurstFrames <= 0 {
773			return fmt.Errorf("scheduler.pacing_burst_frames must be > 0 when pacing is enabled, got %g", s.PacingBurstFrames)
774		}
775		if s.WeightRTTFloor <= 0 {
776			return fmt.Errorf("scheduler.weight_rtt_floor must be > 0 under the weighted policy, got %s", s.WeightRTTFloor)
777		}
778		if s.WeightLossFloor <= 0 {
779			return fmt.Errorf("scheduler.weight_loss_floor must be > 0 under the weighted policy, got %g", s.WeightLossFloor)
780		}
781		return nil
782	}

784	// validate enforces the required-field invariants, failing on the first problem.
785	func (c *Config) validate() error {
786		if !c.Role.valid() {
787			return fmt.Errorf("role must be %q or %q, got %q", RoleEdge, RoleConcentrator, c.Role)
788		}
789		if len(c.Paths) == 0 {
790			return errors.New("at least one path is required")
791		}
792		seen := make(map[string]struct{}, len(c.Paths))
793		// seenSrc maps an already-claimed source_addr to the path that claimed it, so a
794		// second path reusing it can be rejected at LOAD naming both conflicting paths.
795		// The multipath bind Opens each path on (source_addr, listen_port), so two paths
796		// sharing a source_addr collide EADDRINUSE at the second ListenUDP (and on every
797		// re-Open after Down/Up, since the engine passes the fixed port back) — a
798		// misconfiguration that must fail fast at load, not at bring-up (defect D10).
799		seenSrc := make(map[netip.Addr]string, len(c.Paths))
800		for i, p := range c.Paths {
801			if p.Name == "" {
802				return fmt.Errorf("path %d: name is required", i)
803			}
804			if _, dup := seen[p.Name]; dup {
805				return fmt.Errorf("duplicate path name %q", p.Name)
806			}
807			seen[p.Name] = struct{}{}
808			if !p.SourceAddr.IsValid() {
809				return fmt.Errorf("path %q: source_addr is required", p.Name)
810			}
811			// Compare the UNMAPPED form: "192.0.2.10" (Is4) and "::ffff:192.0.2.10"
812			// (Is4In6) are distinct under netip == yet bind the identical v4 socket, so
813			// the two spellings must collide in the guard exactly as they do at bind.
814			src := p.SourceAddr.Unmap()
815			if prev, dup := seenSrc[src]; dup {
816				return fmt.Errorf("paths %q and %q share source_addr %s; each path must bind a distinct source address (a shared source collides EADDRINUSE at the second bind)", prev, p.Name, p.SourceAddr)
817			}
818			seenSrc[src] = p.Name
819		}
820		if !c.WireGuard.PrivateKey.IsSet() {
821			return errors.New("wireguard.private_key is required")
822		}
823		if len(c.WireGuard.Peers) == 0 {
824			return errors.New("at least one wireguard peer is required")
825		}
826		for i, peer := range c.WireGuard.Peers {
827			if !peer.PublicKey.IsSet() {
828				return fmt.Errorf("wireguard peer %d: public_key is required", i)
829			}
830			if c.Role == RoleEdge && len(peer.Endpoints) == 0 {
831				return fmt.Errorf("wireguard peer %d: endpoint is required for the edge role", i)
832			}
833			// The concentrator learns the edge's endpoint dynamically (roaming across
834			// NAT rebinds); an ordered failover endpoint list is meaningless for it, so
835			// reject rather than silently ignore a mis-targeted config (Q18 is edge-side
836			// only).
837			if c.Role == RoleConcentrator && len(peer.Endpoints) != 0 {
838				return fmt.Errorf("wireguard peer %d: endpoint/endpoints is not meaningful for the concentrator role (it learns the edge's endpoint dynamically)", i)
839			}
840		}
841		if c.Role == RoleConcentrator && c.WireGuard.ListenPort == 0 {
842			return errors.New("wireguard.listen_port is required for the concentrator role")
843		}
844		if !c.PSK.IsSet() {
845			return errors.New("psk is required (authenticates outer control/probe frames)")
846		}
847		if err := c.Amnezia.validate(); err != nil {
848			return err
849		}
850		if err := c.Scheduler.validate(); err != nil {
851			return err
852		}
853		if err := c.FEC.validate(); err != nil {
854			return err
855		}
856		return nil
857	}
858	
859	// validate enforces the amnezia obfuscation invariants (defect D1). An
860	// unconfigured block is valid (plain WireGuard). A configured block must specify
861	// the WHOLE junk/size set and carry a consistent magic-header set, so a partial
862	// or inconsistent obfuscation profile FAILS FAST at load rather than producing a
863	// silently mismatched tunnel that never handshakes.
864	//
865	// This runs after applyDefaults, so an omitted magic-header set has already been
866	// filled with the standard 1..4 values; the header check below therefore only
867	// fires for a genuinely partial header set (some given, some left zero).
868	func (a Amnezia) validate() error {
869		if !a.Configured() {
870			return nil
871		}
872		// All-or-nothing: enabling amnezia at all requires the full junk/size set, so
873		// both ends are forced to specify the same complete profile. A partial block
874		// (e.g. jc/jmin/jmax set but s1/s2 omitted) would leave the ends deriving
875		// different profiles and the handshake would fail closed with no diagnostic.
876		if a.Jc <= 0 || a.Jmin <= 0 || a.Jmax <= 0 || a.S1 <= 0 || a.S2 <= 0 {
877			return fmt.Errorf("amnezia: incomplete obfuscation set — when configured, jc, jmin, jmax, s1 and s2 must all be > 0 (got jc=%d jmin=%d jmax=%d s1=%d s2=%d)",
878				a.Jc, a.Jmin, a.Jmax, a.S1, a.S2)
879		}
880		if a.Jmin > a.Jmax {
881			return fmt.Errorf("amnezia: require jmin <= jmax, got jmin=%d jmax=%d", a.Jmin, a.Jmax)
882		}
883		// Junk sizes must not collide: amneziawg-go classifies an incoming datagram's
884		// message type by its on-the-wire length, so the obfuscated initiation packet
885		// (MessageInitiationSize + s1) and response packet (MessageResponseSize + s2)
886		// must differ in length. A colliding s1/s2 pair passes config load but is
887		// rejected later by the engine's IpcSet ("new init size == new response size");
888		// reject it here so the collision fails fast at load, at the right locus. The
889		// engine's own MessageInitiationSize (148) and MessageResponseSize (92)
890		// constants are used so this check tracks the vendored fork if it ever changes.
891		if awgdevice.MessageInitiationSize+a.S1 == awgdevice.MessageResponseSize+a.S2 {
892			return fmt.Errorf("amnezia: junk sizes collide — obfuscated init size %d (%d+s1) must differ from response size %d (%d+s2); adjust s1/s2",
893				awgdevice.MessageInitiationSize+a.S1, awgdevice.MessageInitiationSize,
894				awgdevice.MessageResponseSize+a.S2, awgdevice.MessageResponseSize)
895		}
896		// Magic headers must be distinct: the receive path classifies a datagram's
897		// message type by its header value, so two equal headers make two message
898		// types indistinguishable. An all-zero header set is left for applyDefaults;
899		// any non-zero header requires a complete, distinct set (a partial set leaves
900		// zeros here and is caught as a duplicate).
901		if a.H1 != 0 || a.H2 != 0 || a.H3 != 0 || a.H4 != 0 {
902			if a.H1 == a.H2 || a.H1 == a.H3 || a.H1 == a.H4 ||
903				a.H2 == a.H3 || a.H2 == a.H4 || a.H3 == a.H4 {
904				return fmt.Errorf("amnezia: magic headers must be a complete, distinct set, got h1=%d h2=%d h3=%d h4=%d",
905					a.H1, a.H2, a.H3, a.H4)
906			}
907		}
908		return nil
909	}
