1	package reseq
2	
3	import (
4		"net/netip"
5		"sync"
6		"time"
7	)
8	
9	// Clock is the injected time source. Production wires a monotonic wall clock
10	// (SystemClock); tests wire a hand-advanced fake so ordering, window, and
11	// timeout behaviour are deterministic with no real sleeps. It is intentionally
12	// structurally identical to telemetry.Clock so the same fake can drive both.
13	type Clock interface {
14		Now() time.Time
15	}
16	
17	// SystemClock is the production Clock backed by the monotonic wall clock.
18	type SystemClock struct{}
19	
20	// Now returns the current monotonic time.
21	func (SystemClock) Now() time.Time { return time.Now() }
22	
23	// Discontinuity/resync guard tuning. A DATA frame is UNAUTHENTICATED by design
24	// (internal/frame/frame.go: "DATA/PARITY forgeable by design"), so a garbage
25	// datagram decoding as KindData (p ~ 1/256) delivers a uniformly-random uint64
26	// OuterSeq, and a genuine peer PROCESS RESTART resets the peer's outerSeq counter
27	// to 1. Neither may be trusted to move the release point arbitrarily.
28	const (
29		// resyncFactor (K) bounds how far AHEAD of the release point a single
30		// unauthenticated frame is trusted to advance the window as a plausible loss
31		// burst. Under bounded cross-path reorder a legitimate frame sits within one
32		// window of next; a forward jump up to K windows is still treated as a loss
33		// burst (the sound bounded-memory window-advance handles it in O(window)), but
34		// a jump of K windows or more is SUSPECT: a single such frame never advances
35		// next. K=4 leaves generous headroom above the 1-window reorder bound while
36		// capping the release point a single trusted frame can skip to (K-1) windows.
37		resyncFactor = 4
38	
39		// resyncCorroborate (C) is how many out-of-band frames carrying DISTINCT seqs
40		// that mutually span less than one window must be observed before the release
41		// point is re-pinned. A genuine peer restart (1,2,3,...) or a consistent
42		// long-outage forward jump emits connected, distinct seqs that trivially
43		// corroborate. A repeated identical seq does NOT advance the count: a single
44		// junk or forged datagram re-delivered — a network duplicate, or a forger
45		// replaying one datagram — contributes only ONE distinct seq, so it can never
46		// self-corroborate. Corroboration therefore requires C INDEPENDENT junk seqs to
47		// land mutually within one window; each is independent in 2^64, so that occurs
48		// with probability ~(window/2^64)^(C-1) ~ 1e-32 for C=3 and window=2048 — junk
49		// never triggers a resync, while a real discontinuity resyncs after losing at
50		// most C-1 frames. Any in-band (near-current) frame resets the run, so ordinary
51		// traffic interspersed with rare junk never accumulates a false corroboration.
52		resyncCorroborate = 3
53	)
54	
55	// Item is one released inner datagram plus the outer source address of the DATA
56	// frame that carried it. The source travels with the payload so the multipath
57	// Bind can pin its single virtual endpoint to the FIRST delivered frame's
58	// source even when that frame was buffered and released out of arrival order.
59	type Item struct {
60		Payload []byte
61		Src     netip.AddrPort
62	}
63	
64	// slot is one ring cell. A live cell (occupied) holds exactly one buffered DATA
65	// frame whose OuterSeq maps to this index; seq is retained so a stale cell (an
66	// already-drained seq that shares the index modulo window) is never mistaken for
67	// a live one.
68	type slot struct {
69		seq      uint64
70		src      netip.AddrPort
71		payload  []byte
72		occupied bool
73	}
74	
75	// Resequencer is a bounded-window, timeout-based receive resequencing buffer. It
76	// consumes DATA frames tagged with the multipath codec's OWN outer sequence
77	// number (frame.Data.OuterSeq — never WireGuard's inner counter) arriving out of
78	// order across paths, and releases the decoded inner datagrams in strictly
79	// ascending outer-seq order before they reach the WireGuard engine. This absorbs
80	// multipath reorder entirely in the outer layer so WG's RFC 6479 anti-replay
81	// filter (window 8128, see docs/p0-findings.md §6) only ever sees monotonically
82	// increasing inner counters and never drops a legitimately-delivered packet.
83	//
84	// Guarantees (see the package tests):
85	//   - Ordering: released frames are strictly ascending in outer-seq. A frame
86	//     whose seq is below the release point is dropped, never delivered late, so
87	//     delivery is monotonic and WG sees no replay-window regression.
88	//   - Exactly-once: duplicates (same seq re-observed before or after release) are
89	//     dropped; every deliverable frame is released exactly once.
90	//   - Bounded memory: at most `window` frames are buffered. A frame at or beyond
91	//     next+window forces the window to advance (releasing/skipping the tail),
92	//     so an adversarial reorder/loss trace cannot grow the buffer without bound.
93	//   - Timeout progress: when the head-of-line seq is missing, buffered frames
94	//     ahead of the gap are held at most `timeout`; then the gap is skipped
95	//     (treated as lost) and the run released, rather than stalling forever. Each
96	//     distinct head-of-line gap gets its OWN full timeout.
97	//   - Bounded compute: every Observe runs in O(window). No frame — however wild
98	//     its (unauthenticated, possibly forged) seq — can make the release point
99	//     advance one seq at a time across a gap of up to 2^64 under the mutex.
100	//   - Discontinuity resilience: an unauthenticated frame whose seq lies farther
101	//     than resyncFactor*window from next never moves the release point on its own.
102	//     A genuine peer restart or long outage — a run of frames carrying DISTINCT
103	//     seqs that mutually fall within one window — re-pins (resyncs) the release
104	//     point within a bounded number of frames; uniformly-random junk seqs, and a
105	//     single junk/forged seq re-delivered any number of times, do not.
106	//
107	// Concurrency: every method is guarded by one mutex, so the shared instance is
108	// safe for the multipath Bind's per-path receive goroutines. The mutex is held
109	// only for in-memory bookkeeping — never across a syscall — so it does not
110	// perturb the Bind's lock-free virtual-endpoint fast path or its
111	// syscall-outside-mutex send discipline.
112	//
113	// FEC seam (T24): Observe is the sole ingestion point and is keyed purely by
114	// outer-seq. An FEC decoder inserted on the receive path recovers missing DATA
115	// frames from PARITY and feeds each recovered frame's ORIGINAL outer-seq through
116	// Observe, identically to a natively-received frame — so FEC recovery slots in
117	// BEFORE resequencing with no change to this type.
118	type Resequencer struct {
119		window  uint64
120		timeout time.Duration
121		clock   Clock
122	
123		mu      sync.Mutex
124		started bool   // false until the first Observe pins the initial next
125		next    uint64 // lowest outer-seq not yet released
126		ring    []slot // len == window; indexed by seq % window
127		buf     int    // number of occupied slots
128	
129		ready    []Item // FIFO of released items awaiting Pop
130		readyPos int    // read cursor into ready (front of the FIFO)
131	
132		// Head-of-line timeout: when next is a gap but frames ahead are buffered,
133		// waiting is armed with a deadline measured from when the gap first formed.
134		waiting  bool
135		deadline time.Time
136	
137		// Discontinuity/resync guard: the current run of out-of-band (suspect) frames.
138		// resyncSeqs holds the DISTINCT suspect seqs seen in the run (a repeated
139		// identical seq does not advance corroboration); [resyncLo, resyncHi] is the
140		// run's seq span. When resyncSeqs reaches resyncCorroborate distinct seqs the
141		// run is deemed a real discontinuity and the release point re-pins (see
142		// tryResync). It holds at most resyncCorroborate seqs, so memory stays bounded.
143		resyncSeqs []uint64
144		resyncLo   uint64
145		resyncHi   uint64
146	
147		// Diagnostics (read via the accessors; useful for the bounded-memory asserts).
148		highWater   int    // max occupied slots ever held
149		dropDup     uint64 // frames dropped as duplicates
150		dropLate    uint64 // frames dropped as already-past the release point
151		dropSuspect uint64 // out-of-band frames dropped while (not yet) corroborating
152		skipped     uint64 // seqs skipped (treated lost) by window-advance or timeout
153		releasedN   uint64 // frames released for delivery
154		resyncs     uint64 // release-point re-pins after a corroborated discontinuity
155		rebaselines uint64 // release-point re-baselines forced by a trusted control event (hub failover)
156	}
157	
158	// New returns a resequencer buffering at most window outer-seq positions and
159	// holding a head-of-line-blocked run for at most timeout before skipping the gap.
160	// window must be positive; timeout may be zero (a missing head seq is skipped as
161	// soon as the next Observe/Pop advances the clock past the arrival instant).
162	func New(window uint64, timeout time.Duration, clock Clock) *Resequencer {
163		if window == 0 {
164			panic("reseq: window must be positive")
165		}
166		if clock == nil {
167			panic("reseq: clock must be non-nil")
168		}
169		return &Resequencer{
170			window:  window,
171			timeout: timeout,
172			clock:   clock,
173			ring:    make([]slot, window),
174		}
175	}
176	
177	// Observe ingests one DATA frame's inner payload under its outer-seq. It takes
178	// ownership of payload (the caller must not mutate it afterwards); the multipath
179	// Bind passes the freshly-decoded frame.Data.Payload, which aliases nothing else.
180	// src is the outer source address the frame arrived from. Any frames that become
181	// deliverable are appended to the ready FIFO for Pop.
182	func (r *Resequencer) Observe(seq uint64, payload []byte, src netip.AddrPort) {
183		now := r.clock.Now()
184		r.mu.Lock()
185		defer r.mu.Unlock()
186	
187		if !r.started {
188			r.started = true
189			r.next = seq
190		}
191	
192		// Give up on any head-of-line gap whose hold time has elapsed before deciding
193		// where this frame lands.
194		r.expire(now)
195	
196		// Classify the frame against the release window and the discontinuity guard.
197		// admit adjusts next (window-advance or resync) when warranted and reports
198		// whether the frame now lands inside [next, next+window) and should be placed.
199		if !r.admit(seq) {
200			return
201		}
202	
203		cell := &r.ring[seq%r.window]
204		if cell.occupied && cell.seq == seq {
205			r.dropDup++
206			return
207		}
208		cell.seq = seq
209		cell.src = src
210		cell.payload = payload
211		cell.occupied = true
212		r.buf++
213		if r.buf > r.highWater {
214			r.highWater = r.buf
215		}
216	
217		r.drain()
218		r.arm(now)
219	}
220	
221	// ObserveRecovered ingests a payload the FEC decoder reconstructed from parity (T24).
222	// Unlike Observe it NEVER moves or re-pins the release point: a recovered frame is a
223	// REPAIR for a past gap, so if it has already fallen below the release point it is
224	// dropped as too-late, and it can neither window-advance nor corroborate a resync — so
225	// FEC recovery can never CAUSE the burst loss it exists to prevent (a late batch of K
226	// recovered seqs below `next` must not be able to dump the live buffer via a backward
227	// resync). It returns whether the frame was PLACED (landed at or above the release
228	// point and will be delivered in order); a dropped-late recovered frame returns false,
229	// which the datapath uses to keep the /metrics recovered counter honest — counting
230	// frames actually delivered ahead of the release point, not merely reconstructed.
231	func (r *Resequencer) ObserveRecovered(seq uint64, payload []byte, src netip.AddrPort) bool {
232		now := r.clock.Now()
233		r.mu.Lock()
234		defer r.mu.Unlock()
235	
236		if !r.started {
237			r.started = true
238			r.next = seq
239		}
240		r.expire(now)
241	
242		// The placement window is EXACTLY [next, next+window). A recovered frame below next
243		// is too late (drop, and — crucially — never feed the resync guard). One at or beyond
244		// next+window would require a window-advance we refuse to perform for a reconstructed
245		// frame, since that would skip live seqs on the strength of a repair. Neither branch
246		// touches next or the corroboration run.
247		if seq < r.next {
248			if r.next-seq > r.window {
249				r.dropSuspect++
250			} else {
251				r.dropLate++
252			}
253			return false
254		}
255		if seq-r.next >= r.window {
256			r.dropLate++
257			return false
258		}
259	
260		cell := &r.ring[seq%r.window]
261		if cell.occupied && cell.seq == seq {
262			r.dropDup++
263			return false // already buffered (a received frame or a prior recovery)
264		}
265		cell.seq = seq
266		cell.src = src
267		cell.payload = payload
268		cell.occupied = true
269		r.buf++
270		if r.buf > r.highWater {
271			r.highWater = r.buf
272		}
273	
274		r.drain()
275		r.arm(now)
276		return true
277	}
278	
279	// admit classifies seq against the release window and the discontinuity guard and
280	// decides whether the frame should be buffered. It returns true when seq now lies
281	// inside [next, next+window) and should be placed; false (having bumped the
282	// appropriate drop counter) when the frame is dropped. It may advance next by a
283	// bounded amount on a plausible loss burst, or re-pin next on a corroborated
284	// discontinuity. Caller holds r.mu.
285	func (r *Resequencer) admit(seq uint64) bool {
286		if seq < r.next {
287			// Below the release point. A frame more than one window below next is
288			// impossibly late under bounded reorder — treat it as SUSPECT (a peer
289			// restart resets outerSeq to 1, so its frames land here). A frame within a
290			// window below next is an ordinary straggler/duplicate.
291			if r.next-seq > r.window {
292				if r.tryResync(seq) {
293					return true
294				}
295				r.dropSuspect++
296				return false
297			}
298			r.resyncReset() // near-current traffic: not a discontinuity
299			r.dropLate++
300			return false
301		}
302		// seq >= next.
303		if seq-r.next >= resyncFactor*r.window {
304			// Too far ahead to be a plausible loss burst — SUSPECT (garbage-decoded or
305			// forged high seq). A single such frame must not advance next.
306			if r.tryResync(seq) {
307				return true
308			}
309			r.dropSuspect++
310			return false
311		}
312		// In-window, or a moderate (plausible loss-burst) forward jump: legitimate
313		// near-current traffic, so any in-progress discontinuity run is broken.
314		r.resyncReset()
315		if seq-r.next >= r.window {
316			// Beyond the window: advance the release point so the frame fits at the top
317			// of the window, releasing buffered frames below the new base in order and
318			// skipping the (assumed lost) gaps. This is what bounds memory.
319			r.advanceTo(seq - r.window + 1)
320		}
321		return true
322	}
323	
324	// Pop returns the next in-order released inner datagram, or ok=false when none is
325	// ready. It also advances the head-of-line timeout, so a receive goroutine that
326	// only drains still makes timeout progress.
327	func (r *Resequencer) Pop() (Item, bool) {
328		now := r.clock.Now()
329		r.mu.Lock()
330		defer r.mu.Unlock()
331	
332		r.expire(now)
333	
334		if r.readyPos >= len(r.ready) {
335			return Item{}, false
336		}
337		it := r.ready[r.readyPos]
338		r.ready[r.readyPos] = Item{} // release the payload reference
339		r.readyPos++
340		if r.readyPos >= len(r.ready) {
341			// FIFO drained: reset to reuse the backing array, keeping it bounded.
342			r.ready = r.ready[:0]
343			r.readyPos = 0
344		}
345		return it, true
346	}
347	
348	// drain releases the contiguous run starting at next into the ready FIFO. Caller
349	// holds r.mu.
350	func (r *Resequencer) drain() {
351		for {
352			cell := &r.ring[r.next%r.window]
353			if !cell.occupied || cell.seq != r.next {
354				return
355			}
356			r.release(cell)
357			r.next++
358		}
359	}
360	
361	// advanceTo moves next up to target, releasing any occupied cell it passes (in
362	// ascending order) and counting the empty positions as skipped (lost) seqs. It is
363	// O(window), NEVER O(target-next): all occupied cells live in [next, next+window),
364	// so once next reaches next+window the remaining gap is provably empty and is
365	// closed by arithmetic rather than iterated. Caller holds r.mu; target must be
366	// > next.
367	func (r *Resequencer) advanceTo(target uint64) {
368		// Only [next, next+window) can hold occupied cells; iterate at most that far.
369		limit := target
370		if limit > r.next+r.window {
371			limit = r.next + r.window
372		}
373		for r.next < limit {
374			cell := &r.ring[r.next%r.window]
375			if cell.occupied && cell.seq == r.next {
376				r.release(cell)
377			} else {
378				r.skipped++
379			}
380			r.next++
381		}
382		if r.next < target {
383			// The remaining gap [next, target) is entirely empty — no occupied cell can
384			// live a full window ahead of the old release point. Close it by arithmetic.
385			r.skipped += target - r.next
386			r.next = target
387		}
388	}
389	
390	// release moves one occupied cell's payload into the ready FIFO and clears it.
391	// Caller holds r.mu.
392	func (r *Resequencer) release(cell *slot) {
393		r.ready = append(r.ready, Item{Payload: cell.payload, Src: cell.src})
394		cell.occupied = false
395		cell.payload = nil
396		r.buf--
397		r.releasedN++
398	}
399	
400	// expire skips a head-of-line gap whose hold time has elapsed: it jumps next to
401	// the smallest buffered seq (treating the intervening seqs as lost) and releases
402	// the now-contiguous run. Caller holds r.mu.
403	func (r *Resequencer) expire(now time.Time) {
404		if !r.waiting || now.Before(r.deadline) {
405			return
406		}
407		minSeq, ok := r.smallestBuffered()
408		if !ok {
409			r.waiting = false
410			return
411		}
412		// Skip the lost gap [next, minSeq), then release from minSeq onward.
413		for r.next < minSeq {
414			r.skipped++
415			r.next++
416		}
417		r.drain()
418		// Clear the armed state BEFORE re-arming so that a distinct SECOND gap exposed
419		// by this release gets its own full timeout rather than inheriting this gap's
420		// already-elapsed deadline (which would skip it with ~zero hold).
421		r.waiting = false
422		r.arm(now)
423	}
424	
425	// arm (re)evaluates the head-of-line timeout after a change. When next is a gap
426	// but buffered frames sit ahead of it, it starts the hold clock (only if not
427	// already running, so the deadline measures from when the gap first formed).
428	// Otherwise it disarms. Caller holds r.mu.
429	func (r *Resequencer) arm(now time.Time) {
430		cell := &r.ring[r.next%r.window]
431		headPresent := cell.occupied && cell.seq == r.next
432		if !headPresent && r.buf > 0 {
433			if !r.waiting {
434				r.waiting = true
435				r.deadline = now.Add(r.timeout)
436			}
437		} else {
438			r.waiting = false
439		}
440	}
441	
442	// tryResync feeds one out-of-band (suspect) seq into the discontinuity guard. It
443	// re-pins the release point ONLY after resyncCorroborate DISTINCT suspect seqs
444	// whose values mutually span less than one window (a genuine peer restart or long
445	// outage emits connected, distinct seqs that corroborate; uniformly-random junk
446	// seqs, each independent in 2^64, do not, and a single junk/forged seq re-delivered
447	// contributes only ONE distinct value so it can never self-corroborate). It
448	// returns true and performs the resync when the corroboration threshold is met —
449	// re-pinning next to the triggering seq so the caller places it as the new head
450	// and delivery resumes immediately, without a phantom gap or an extra timeout —
451	// and false while still collecting or when the seq fails to corroborate the
452	// current run. Caller holds r.mu.
453	func (r *Resequencer) tryResync(seq uint64) bool {
454		if len(r.resyncSeqs) == 0 || r.spanExceeds(seq) {
455			// Start (or restart) the run at this seq: it does not corroborate the
456			// current run, so it becomes the anchor of a fresh one.
457			r.resyncLo, r.resyncHi = seq, seq
458			r.resyncSeqs = append(r.resyncSeqs[:0], seq)
459			return false
460		}
461		if r.runContains(seq) {
462			// A repeated identical seq does NOT advance corroboration: a lone junk or
463			// forged datagram re-delivered must not count as independent corroboration.
464			return false
465		}
466		if seq < r.resyncLo {
467			r.resyncLo = seq
468		}
469		if seq > r.resyncHi {
470			r.resyncHi = seq
471		}
472		r.resyncSeqs = append(r.resyncSeqs, seq)
473		if len(r.resyncSeqs) < resyncCorroborate {
474			return false
475		}
476		// Corroborated discontinuity: re-pin to the triggering seq and resume delivery.
477		r.resync(seq)
478		return true
479	}
480	
481	// runContains reports whether seq is already one of the distinct suspect seqs in
482	// the current corroboration run. The run holds at most resyncCorroborate seqs, so
483	// this scan is O(C). Caller holds r.mu.
484	func (r *Resequencer) runContains(seq uint64) bool {
485		for _, s := range r.resyncSeqs {
486			if s == seq {
487				return true
488			}
489		}
490		return false
491	}
492	
493	// spanExceeds reports whether adding seq to the current corroboration run would
494	// make its seq span reach or exceed one window (i.e. seq does not mutually fall
495	// within one window of the run so far). Caller holds r.mu.
496	func (r *Resequencer) spanExceeds(seq uint64) bool {
497		lo, hi := r.resyncLo, r.resyncHi
498		if seq < lo {
499			lo = seq
500		}
501		if seq > hi {
502			hi = seq
503		}
504		return hi-lo >= r.window
505	}
506	
507	// resync re-pins the release point to base after a confirmed discontinuity,
508	// discarding all buffered frames (they belong to the pre-discontinuity stream) in
509	// O(window). The already-released FIFO is untouched — those were legitimate prior
510	// deliveries. Caller holds r.mu.
511	func (r *Resequencer) resync(base uint64) {
512		for i := range r.ring {
513			r.ring[i] = slot{}
514		}
515		r.buf = 0
516		r.next = base
517		r.waiting = false
518		r.resyncReset()
519		r.resyncs++
520	}
521	
522	// resyncReset abandons the current corroboration run. Caller holds r.mu.
523	func (r *Resequencer) resyncReset() {
524		r.resyncSeqs = r.resyncSeqs[:0]
525		r.resyncLo = 0
526		r.resyncHi = 0
527	}
528	
529	// Rebaseline discards the buffered (pre-switch) frames and UNPINS the release
530	// point so the NEXT Observe re-anchors `next` to that frame's outer-seq, exactly as
531	// if the stream had just started. It exists for the edge-side HUB FAILOVER (T57): a
532	// concentrator switch changes the DATA-frame SENDER, and the standby is a separate
533	// process whose outer-seq restarts near 1 — far below the release point the prior
534	// hub's high-rate stream advanced `next` to. The unauthenticated tryResync path
535	// cannot rescue this in time: it needs resyncCorroborate (3) distinct low seqs
536	// within one window, but a freshly re-handshaking standby emits only ~1 DATA frame
537	// (the handshake response) per RekeyTimeout, so corroboration falls outside the
538	// failover window and the response is dropped as SUSPECT — the tunnel never
539	// re-establishes (defect D32). Unlike a DATA frame (forgeable by design, hence the
540	// corroboration guard), THIS re-pin is driven by a TRUSTED control event — the
541	// operator-configured ordered-endpoint switch, above the unauthenticated wire — so
542	// re-anchoring on the next single frame is sound. Buffered frames (from the dead
543	// prior hub) are discarded in O(window); the already-released FIFO of prior
544	// legitimate deliveries is untouched. Idempotent and safe before the first Observe
545	// (started stays false). Takes r.mu; the caller must NOT hold it.
546	func (r *Resequencer) Rebaseline() {
547		r.mu.Lock()
548		defer r.mu.Unlock()
549		for i := range r.ring {
550			r.ring[i] = slot{}
551		}
552		r.buf = 0
553		r.started = false
554		r.waiting = false
555		r.resyncReset()
556		r.rebaselines++
557	}
558	
559	// smallestBuffered scans the ring for the lowest occupied seq at or above next.
560	// All occupied seqs lie in [next, next+window), so this is bounded by window and
561	// runs only on the (rare) timeout path. Caller holds r.mu.
562	func (r *Resequencer) smallestBuffered() (uint64, bool) {
563		best := uint64(0)
564		found := false
565		for i := range r.ring {
566			c := &r.ring[i]
567			if c.occupied && c.seq >= r.next && (!found || c.seq < best) {
568				best = c.seq
569				found = true
570			}
571		}
572		return best, found
573	}
574	
575	// Buffered reports the number of frames currently held (not yet released). It is
576	// always <= window, which the bounded-memory tests assert.
577	func (r *Resequencer) Buffered() int {
578		r.mu.Lock()
579		defer r.mu.Unlock()
580		return r.buf
581	}
582	
583	// Pending reports the number of released-but-not-yet-popped items in the FIFO.
584	func (r *Resequencer) Pending() int {
585		r.mu.Lock()
586		defer r.mu.Unlock()
587		return len(r.ready) - r.readyPos
588	}
589	
590	// HighWater reports the maximum number of frames ever buffered simultaneously.
591	func (r *Resequencer) HighWater() int {
592		r.mu.Lock()
593		defer r.mu.Unlock()
594		return r.highWater
595	}
596	
597	// Stats is a snapshot of the resequencer's cumulative counters.
598	type Stats struct {
599		Released       uint64 // frames released for delivery
600		DroppedDup     uint64 // frames dropped as duplicates
601		DroppedOld     uint64 // frames dropped as already-past the release point
602		DroppedSuspect uint64 // out-of-band frames dropped while (not yet) corroborating
603		Skipped        uint64 // seqs skipped (lost) by window-advance or timeout
604		Resyncs        uint64 // release-point re-pins after a corroborated discontinuity
605		Rebaselines    uint64 // release-point re-baselines forced by a trusted control event (hub failover)
606	}
607	
608	// Stats returns a snapshot of the cumulative counters.
609	func (r *Resequencer) Stats() Stats {
610		r.mu.Lock()
611		defer r.mu.Unlock()
612		return Stats{
613			Released:       r.releasedN,
614			DroppedDup:     r.dropDup,
615			DroppedOld:     r.dropLate,
616			DroppedSuspect: r.dropSuspect,
617			Skipped:        r.skipped,
618			Resyncs:        r.resyncs,
619			Rebaselines:    r.rebaselines,
620		}
621	}
622

Let me confirm the device wiring and the telemetry PSK plane to ground the demux-bootstrap and per-peer-PSK questions.

1	// Package device brings a wanbond tunnel up from a validated configuration: it
2	// creates the TUN interface, drives the embedded amneziawg-go engine over the
3	// multipath Bind, and applies the WireGuard (and, when configured, amnezia
4	// obfuscation) parameters via the engine's UAPI. It owns ONLY the tunnel engine
5	// — interface addressing and routing are left to the operator (systemd/wg-quick
6	// style), so the daemon stays free of privileged shell-outs. The interface name
7	// is exposed via Tunnel.Name for that external configuration step.
8	package device
9	
10	import (
11		"context"
12		"crypto/rand"
13		"encoding/hex"
14		"fmt"
15		"reflect"
16		"strings"
17		"sync"
18		"time"
19	
20		awgdevice "github.com/amnezia-vpn/amneziawg-go/device"
21		"github.com/amnezia-vpn/amneziawg-go/tun"
22	
23		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/adaptivefec"
24		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/bind"
25		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/config"
26		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/fec"
27		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/log"
28		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/metrics"
29		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/sched"
30		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/telemetry"
31	)
32	
33	// metricsShutdownTimeout bounds the graceful shutdown of the /metrics endpoint on
34	// Close (and on a reload that rebinds it). The endpoint is loopback-only with no
35	// long-lived scrapes, so this is comfortably generous.
36	const metricsShutdownTimeout = 2 * time.Second
37	
38	// defaultTUNName is the requested interface name; the kernel honours it unless it
39	// collides (it never does across the edge and concentrator network namespaces).
40	const defaultTUNName = "wanbond0"
41	
42	// tunMTU is the tunnel (TUN) MTU for a config: the default path MTU minus the
43	// IP+UDP, outer DATA-frame, and WireGuard transport overheads, so a full-MTU inner
44	// packet never fragments on the wire (see internal/bind mtu.go and docs/p1-mtu.md).
45	// This is smaller than the plain-WireGuard 1420 because the bonding layer adds its
46	// own outer DATA frame per datagram; with FEC enabled it is a further
47	// bind.FECParityMTUPenalty smaller so a full-size PARITY frame also fits the path MTU
48	// (T24) rather than fragmenting.
49	func tunMTU(cfg *config.Config) int {
50		return bind.InnerMTU(bind.DefaultPathMTU, cfg.FEC.Enabled)
51	}
52	
53	// Tunnel is a running wanbond tunnel: the amneziawg engine, its TUN, and the
54	// multipath Bind. Close tears all three down.
55	type Tunnel struct {
56		dev  *awgdevice.Device
57		tun  tun.Device
58		name string
59		// bind is the multipath Bind the engine drives; Reload calls its runtime
60		// AddPath/RemovePath to apply a config-reload path diff (T30).
61		bind *bind.Multipath
62		// cfg is the configuration the RUNNING tunnel reflects: the boot config, its path
63		// set updated on each successful Reload to the current membership (survivors keep
64		// their original parameters — a membership-only reload does not re-apply modified
65		// path parameters or non-path fields). Reload diffs the next desired config against
66		// it to warn about changes it cannot apply. Guarded by reloadMu.
67		cfg *config.Config
68		// reloadMu serializes Reload against itself (SIGHUP handlers), guarding cfg.
69		reloadMu sync.Mutex
70		// log is the device-component logger, retained for Reload diagnostics.
71		log log.Logger
72		// stopProbes halts the per-path probe cadence loop (T37). Close calls it before
73		// tearing the engine down so emission stops before the sockets close. It is
74		// idempotent and never nil (a no-op when the bind runs without probers).
75		stopProbes func()
76		// stopReconcile halts the background deferred-path reconcile loop (T55): it retries
77		// binding the paths a tolerant Open left DOWN as their addresses appear. Close calls
78		// it before tearing the engine down so no reconcile races the socket close. It is
79		// idempotent and never nil (a no-op when the bind runs without probers / never defers).
80		stopReconcile func()
81		// stopHubFailover halts the edge-side hub-failover monitor loop (T57): it watches the
82		// per-path liveness plane and, on hub loss (all paths to the active concentrator DOWN),
83		// advances to the next ordered peer endpoint, repoints the bond's remote, and initiates
84		// a fresh WG re-handshake. Close calls it before tearing the engine down. It is
85		// idempotent and never nil (a no-op on the concentrator, or an edge with a single
86		// concentrator endpoint, or a bind without the probe transport).
87		stopHubFailover func()
88		// amnezia is the obfuscation profile this tunnel holds against the
89		// process-global amnezia guard (see amneziaGuard); Close releases it.
90		amnezia     config.Amnezia
91		releaseOnce sync.Once
92	
93		// metricsSrc is the live metrics.Source over the Bind; it is stable for the tunnel's
94		// life (the Bind pointer never changes), so a reload that rebinds the endpoint reuses
95		// the SAME Source — its derived-throughput last-sample state survives the rebind.
96		metricsSrc metrics.Source
97		// metricsSrv is the running /metrics endpoint, nil when [metrics].listen is empty.
98		// It is (re)assigned by applyMetricsLocked and read by Close; both hold reloadMu, so
99		// a SIGHUP-driven rebind never races shutdown. metricsListen mirrors the address it
100		// is bound to so a reload can detect a listen change without inspecting the server.
101		metricsSrv    *metrics.Server
102		metricsListen string
103	}
104	
105	// SINGLE-ENGINE-PER-PROCESS INVARIANT (defect D2).
106	//
107	// amneziawg-go stores the amnezia magic-header message types in PACKAGE-GLOBAL
108	// variables — device.MessageInitiationType, MessageResponseType,
109	// MessageCookieReplyType, MessageTransportType. (*device.Device).IpcSet assigns
110	// them (in handlePostConfig) from a configured engine's profile, and
111	// (*device.Device).Close restores them to the WireGuard defaults (in
112	// resetProtocol) UNCONDITIONALLY — closing ANY engine, plain or configured,
113	// reverts the process-global message types to their defaults.
114	//
115	// Two consequences make a CONFIGURED (amnezia) engine require PROCESS
116	// EXCLUSIVITY:
117	//   - a second configured engine would overwrite the first engine's message-type
118	//     framing at IpcSet; and even with the SAME profile, closing the first engine
119	//     runs resetProtocol and reverts the globals to defaults under the second,
120	//     still-live engine, silently dropping its tunnel to plain-WireGuard framing.
121	//   - closing ANY other engine — a PLAIN (unconfigured) one included — runs
122	//     resetProtocol and resets the globals out from under a live configured engine.
123	//
124	// So the rule wanbond ASSERTS (rather than vendor-patching the fork) is:
125	//   - a configured engine may come up only when NO other engine is live, and
126	//   - no engine (plain or configured) may come up while a configured engine is live.
127	//
128	// PLAIN engines may coexist with each other: they never set the globals, and
129	// resetProtocol on their Close only restores the defaults they already use, so it
130	// is idempotent among them. wanbond runs exactly one tunnel per process, so the
131	// guard only ever trips on genuine misuse.
132	type amneziaGuard struct {
133		mu         sync.Mutex
134		plainLive  int  // number of live plain-WireGuard (unconfigured) engines
135		configLive bool // whether a configured (amnezia) engine is live (at most one)
136	}
137	
138	// globalAmneziaGuard enforces the single-amnezia-engine-per-process invariant for
139	// every Tunnel brought up in this process.
140	var globalAmneziaGuard amneziaGuard
141	
142	// acquire registers an about-to-start engine against the process-exclusivity rule
143	// (see amneziaGuard). A configured (amnezia) engine is admitted only when NO other
144	// engine is live. A plain engine is admitted only when no configured engine is
145	// live; plain engines may coexist with one another. The caller must release the
146	// SAME profile exactly once when the engine is torn down (plain engines included —
147	// release is no longer a no-op for them).
148	func (g *amneziaGuard) acquire(a config.Amnezia) error {
149		g.mu.Lock()
150		defer g.mu.Unlock()
151		if a.Configured() {
152			if g.configLive || g.plainLive > 0 {
153				return fmt.Errorf("device: refusing to start an amnezia-configured engine while another engine is live: " +
154					"amneziawg-go keeps the magic-header message types in process-global state and resets them to defaults on ANY engine's Close, " +
155					"so a configured engine requires process exclusivity — at most one, with no other engine alongside it (D2)")
156			}
157			g.configLive = true
158			return nil
159		}
160		if g.configLive {
161			return fmt.Errorf("device: refusing to start a second engine while an amnezia-configured engine is live: " +
162				"closing this engine would reset amneziawg-go's process-global message types to defaults under the live amnezia tunnel (D2)")
163		}
164		g.plainLive++
165		return nil
166	}
167	
168	// release drops an engine's hold on the guard. A configured engine clears the
169	// single configured slot; a plain engine decrements the live plain count.
170	func (g *amneziaGuard) release(a config.Amnezia) {
171		g.mu.Lock()
172		defer g.mu.Unlock()
173		if a.Configured() {
174			g.configLive = false
175			return
176		}
177		if g.plainLive > 0 {
178			g.plainLive--
179		}
180	}

182	// Up creates the TUN, wires the multipath Bind into the amneziawg engine,
183	// applies the crypto configuration from cfg, and brings the device up. The same
184	// path drives both roles; the role only changes which UAPI fields cfg carries
185	// (the concentrator sets listen_port; the edge sets each peer's endpoint).
186	func Up(cfg *config.Config, lg log.Logger) (*Tunnel, error) {
187		clg := lg.Component("device")
188	
189		tunDev, err := tun.CreateTUN(defaultTUNName, tunMTU(cfg))
190		if err != nil {
191			return nil, fmt.Errorf("device: create TUN: %w", err)
192		}
193		name, err := tunDev.Name()
194		if err != nil {
195			_ = tunDev.Close()
196			return nil, fmt.Errorf("device: read TUN name: %w", err)
197		}
198	
199		// Claim the single-amnezia-engine-per-process invariant (D2) BEFORE IpcSet
200		// assigns amneziawg-go's process-global message-type state. On any failure
201		// below, ok stays false and the deferred release returns the hold; the
202		// successful path transfers the hold to the returned Tunnel.
203		if err := globalAmneziaGuard.acquire(cfg.Amnezia); err != nil {
204			_ = tunDev.Close()
205			return nil, err
206		}
207		ok := false
208		defer func() {
209			if !ok {
210				globalAmneziaGuard.release(cfg.Amnezia)
211			}
212		}()
213	
214		// One live *telemetry.Prober per path drives on-wire liveness. The SAME prober
215		// values back the scheduler's PathHealth AND the bind's probe transport, so the
216		// liveness the probe loop measures is exactly the liveness the scheduler selects
217		// on (T37 replaces the sched.AlwaysUp placeholder here).
218		scheduler, probers, newProber, err := buildScheduler(cfg, clg)
219		if err != nil {
220			_ = tunDev.Close()
221			return nil, fmt.Errorf("device: build scheduler: %w", err)
222		}
223		mpBind, err := bind.NewMultipath(cfg.Paths, cfg.PSK, scheduler, probers, newProber, fecConfig(cfg.FEC), adaptiveFECConfig(cfg.FEC), cfg.Amnezia)
224		if err != nil {
225			_ = tunDev.Close()
226			return nil, fmt.Errorf("device: build multipath bind: %w", err)
227		}
228		dev := awgdevice.NewDevice(tunDev, mpBind, engineLogger(clg, cfg.Log.Level))
229	
230		uapi, err := uapiConfig(cfg)
231		if err != nil {
232			dev.Close()
233			return nil, fmt.Errorf("device: build UAPI config: %w", err)
234		}
235		if err := dev.IpcSet(uapi); err != nil {
236			dev.Close()
237			return nil, fmt.Errorf("device: apply UAPI config: %w", err)
238		}
239		if err := dev.Up(); err != nil {
240			dev.Close()
241			return nil, fmt.Errorf("device: bring up: %w", err)
242		}
243	
244		// The engine has opened the bind (dev.Up → BindUpdate → Open), so the per-path
245		// sockets exist: start the probe cadence now. Close stops it before dev.Close.
246		// The interval is the SINGLE-SOURCE-OF-TRUTH telemetry default, which also arms
247		// the bind's receive-path liveness sweep throttle (D15).
248		stopProbes := mpBind.StartProbeLoop(telemetry.DefaultProbeInterval)
249		// Start the background deferred-path reconciler alongside probing (T55): a path the
250		// tolerant Open left DOWN (its source_addr not yet assignable — a 5G modem without a
251		// lease at boot) is retried and promoted to a live path as its address appears,
252		// WITHOUT a tunnel restart. Close stops it before dev.Close, like the probe loop.
253		stopReconcile := mpBind.StartReconcileLoop(bind.DefaultReconcileInterval)
254		// Start the edge-side hub-failover monitor (T57): on hub loss (every path to the
255		// active concentrator DOWN) it advances to the next ordered peer endpoint, repoints the
256		// bond's remote, and re-handshakes. A no-op on the concentrator, a single-concentrator
257		// edge, or a bind without the probe transport. Started AFTER dev.Up so the engine peer
258		// the re-handshake looks up exists (IpcSet added it above). Close stops it before
259		// dev.Close, like the probe/reconcile loops.
260		stopHubFailover := startHubFailover(cfg, mpBind, probers, dev, clg)
261	
262		t := &Tunnel{
263			dev: dev, tun: tunDev, name: name, bind: mpBind, cfg: cfg, log: clg,
264			stopProbes: stopProbes, stopReconcile: stopReconcile, stopHubFailover: stopHubFailover, amnezia: cfg.Amnezia,
265			// The Source reads live per-path counters/telemetry from the Bind and derives
266			// throughput from the byte-counter delta between scrapes (see metricsSource). It is
267			// built unconditionally (cheap) so a reload that later turns [metrics].listen ON has
268			// a Source ready; the endpoint itself is started only when a listen is configured.
269			metricsSrc: newMetricsSource(mpBind, telemetry.SystemClock{}),
270		}
271	
272		// Stand up the /metrics endpoint when configured. A non-loopback listen is refused
273		// by metrics.NewServer (fail fast) — surface it as an Up failure rather than booting
274		// a tunnel that silently exposes per-path operational data off-host.
275		t.reloadMu.Lock()
276		err = t.applyMetricsLocked(cfg.Metrics.Listen)
277		t.reloadMu.Unlock()
278		if err != nil {
279			// The tunnel is fully constructed and holds the amnezia guard, so transfer that
280			// ownership to it (ok=true) BEFORE tearing it down: t.Close releases the guard via
281			// releaseOnce, and suppressing the !ok defer avoids a double release. t.Close also
282			// stops the probe loop and closes the engine/TUN.
283			ok = true
284			t.Close()
285			return nil, fmt.Errorf("device: start metrics endpoint: %w", err)
286		}
287	
288		ok = true
289		clg.Info("tunnel up", "interface", name, "role", string(cfg.Role))
290		return t, nil
291	}
292	
293	// applyMetricsLocked reconciles the running /metrics endpoint to listen: it starts the
294	// endpoint when one is desired and none runs, stops it when listen is empty, and rebinds
295	// (stop old, start new) when the address changed. It is idempotent for an unchanged
296	// address. The Source is reused across a rebind so its derived-throughput state is not
297	// reset. The caller MUST hold reloadMu (Up and Reload both do), which also serializes it
298	// against Close reading metricsSrv. On a NewServer/refuse error the previous server is
299	// left running untouched, so a bad reload never drops a working endpoint.
300	func (t *Tunnel) applyMetricsLocked(listen string) error {
301		if listen == t.metricsListen {
302			return nil
303		}
304		if listen == "" {
305			t.stopMetricsLocked()
306			return nil
307		}
308		srv, err := metrics.NewServer(listen, t.metricsSrc, t.log)
309		if err != nil {
310			return err
311		}
312		t.stopMetricsLocked()
313		srv.Start()
314		t.metricsSrv = srv
315		t.metricsListen = listen
316		return nil
317	}
318	
319	// stopMetricsLocked gracefully shuts the running endpoint down (if any) and clears the
320	// bookkeeping. Caller holds reloadMu.
321	func (t *Tunnel) stopMetricsLocked() {
322		if t.metricsSrv == nil {
323			return
324		}
325		ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(context.Background(), metricsShutdownTimeout)
326		defer cancel()
327		if err := t.metricsSrv.Close(ctx); err != nil {
328			t.log.Warn("metrics endpoint shutdown error", "error", err.Error())
329		}
330		t.metricsSrv = nil
331		t.metricsListen = ""
332	}
333	
334	// Reload applies a reloaded configuration to the RUNNING tunnel by diffing its
335	// path set against the live one and adding/removing paths at runtime (T30), WITHOUT
336	// tearing the tunnel down: the WG session, the surviving paths, and in-flight
337	// resequencing are all preserved. cfg is assumed already validated (config.Load
338	// fails fast on a bad file, so a bad reload never reaches here and never disturbs
339	// the running tunnel). Only the path set is diffed — the crypto/amnezia/role fields
340	// are fixed for the life of the process and a reload does not re-key the engine.
341	//
342	// New paths are added BEFORE absent ones are removed, so a failed addition aborts
343	// with every existing path still in service. Diffing is by name: a path present in
344	// both sets is left untouched (its source/dest parameters are not re-read).
345	//
346	// A membership-only reload CANNOT apply every kind of change: a same-name path whose
347	// source/dest changed, a path REORDER (index 0 is the preferred primary, so a reorder
348	// is a priority change), and every non-path field (psk, wireguard, amnezia, role, log,
349	// metrics) are all out of scope for T30. Rather than silently diverge from the file,
350	// Reload logs an EXPLICIT WARNING per ignored change (reloadWarnings) so the operator
351	// knows exactly what was dropped; only the path-membership add/remove is applied.
352	func (t *Tunnel) Reload(cfg *config.Config) error {
353		t.reloadMu.Lock()
354		defer t.reloadMu.Unlock()
355	
356		for _, w := range reloadWarnings(t.cfg, cfg) {
357			t.log.Warn("reload: ignored config change (path-membership reload only)", "change", w)
358		}
359	
360		// Rebind the /metrics endpoint if its listen address changed (T23). This is one of
361		// the few non-path fields a reload DOES apply: the endpoint is external to the WG
362		// session and the Bind, so restarting it disturbs neither. applyMetricsLocked reuses
363		// the tunnel's stable Source (its derived-throughput state survives), and on a refuse
364		// (e.g. a newly non-loopback address) leaves the previous endpoint running and fails
365		// the reload — the running tunnel is never disturbed. Applied BEFORE the path diff so
366		// a metrics-refuse aborts before any membership change.
367		if cfg.Metrics.Listen != t.metricsListen {
368			if err := t.applyMetricsLocked(cfg.Metrics.Listen); err != nil {
369				return fmt.Errorf("device: reload metrics endpoint: %w", err)
370			}
371			t.log.Info("reload: metrics endpoint rebound", "listen", cfg.Metrics.Listen)
372		}
373	
374		add, remove := diffPaths(t.bind.PathNames(), cfg.Paths)
375		for _, def := range add {
376			if err := t.bind.AddPath(def); err != nil {
377				return fmt.Errorf("device: reload add path %q: %w", def.Name, err)
378			}
379			t.log.Info("reload: path added", "path", def.Name)
380		}
381		for _, name := range remove {
382			if err := t.bind.RemovePath(name); err != nil {
383				return fmt.Errorf("device: reload remove path %q: %w", name, err)
384			}
385			t.log.Info("reload: path removed", "path", name)
386		}
387		// Advance the running config to the membership now in service. Survivors keep their
388		// ORIGINAL parameters and all non-path fields stay as booted (the ignored changes
389		// were not applied), so a subsequent identical reload re-warns about a still-diverged
390		// file rather than silently accepting it. Metrics.Listen is carried to the applied
391		// value so a subsequent reload diffs against the endpoint actually running.
392		t.cfg = runningConfig(t.cfg, add, remove)
393		t.cfg.Metrics.Listen = t.metricsListen
394		return nil
395	}
396	
397	// reloadWarnings compares the desired config against the currently-running one and
398	// returns one human-readable warning per change a path-membership-only reload (T30)
399	// cannot apply: a modified same-name path (source/dest), a path reorder, and any
400	// non-path field. Membership add/remove is NOT reported here — it is applied. Full
401	// modify-support is out of scope; SILENCE is not — the operator must be told what was
402	// ignored. It is a pure function so the warning set is unit-testable.
403	func reloadWarnings(live, desired *config.Config) []string {
404		var w []string
405	
406		if live.Role != desired.Role {
407			w = append(w, fmt.Sprintf("role %q -> %q", live.Role, desired.Role))
408		}
409		if !reflect.DeepEqual(live.PSK, desired.PSK) {
410			w = append(w, "psk changed")
411		}
412		if !reflect.DeepEqual(live.WireGuard, desired.WireGuard) {
413			w = append(w, "wireguard section changed")
414		}
415		if !reflect.DeepEqual(live.Amnezia, desired.Amnezia) {
416			w = append(w, "amnezia section changed")
417		}
418		if !reflect.DeepEqual(live.Log, desired.Log) {
419			w = append(w, "log section changed")
420		}
421		// NOTE: a Metrics change is NOT warned here — unlike the other non-path fields, the
422		// reload APPLIES it by rebinding the /metrics endpoint (see Reload). Warning about a
423		// change that is honoured would misinform the operator.
424	
425		// Same-name paths whose parameters changed: diffPaths matches by name only, so a
426		// modified source/dest on an existing path is otherwise silently dropped.
427		liveByName := make(map[string]config.Path, len(live.Paths))
428		for _, p := range live.Paths {
429			liveByName[p.Name] = p
430		}
431		for _, d := range desired.Paths {
432			l, ok := liveByName[d.Name]
433			if !ok {
434				continue // a genuinely new path is added, not ignored
435			}
436			if l.SourceAddr != d.SourceAddr || l.DestAddr != d.DestAddr {
437				w = append(w, fmt.Sprintf("path %q source/dest changed — the running path keeps its original binding", d.Name))
438			}
439		}
440	
441		if reordered(live.Paths, desired.Paths) {
442			w = append(w, "path priority order changed (reorder) — the running order is unchanged")
443		}
444		return w
445	}
446	
447	// reordered reports whether the paths common to BOTH slices appear in a different
448	// relative order in desired than in live. The common subsequence has the same length
449	// on both sides (it is the name intersection), so a positional mismatch is a reorder.
450	func reordered(live, desired []config.Path) bool {
451		desiredNames := make(map[string]struct{}, len(desired))
452		for _, p := range desired {
453			desiredNames[p.Name] = struct{}{}
454		}
455		liveNames := make(map[string]struct{}, len(live))
456		for _, p := range live {
457			liveNames[p.Name] = struct{}{}
458		}
459		var liveCommon, desiredCommon []string
460		for _, p := range live {
461			if _, ok := desiredNames[p.Name]; ok {
462				liveCommon = append(liveCommon, p.Name)
463			}
464		}
465		for _, p := range desired {
466			if _, ok := liveNames[p.Name]; ok {
467				desiredCommon = append(desiredCommon, p.Name)
468			}
469		}
470		for i := range liveCommon {
471			if liveCommon[i] != desiredCommon[i] {
472				return true
473			}
474		}
475		return false
476	}
477	
478	// runningConfig advances the running config to the membership the bind now serves:
479	// the survivors (live paths not removed, keeping their ORIGINAL parameters and order)
480	// followed by the added paths (their new parameters). Non-path fields are carried over
481	// unchanged — a membership-only reload does not re-apply them. It mirrors the m.defs
482	// order the bind rebuilds on a Close→Open (survivors-in-order, then appended adds).
483	func runningConfig(live *config.Config, add []config.Path, remove []string) *config.Config {
484		removeSet := make(map[string]struct{}, len(remove))
485		for _, n := range remove {
486			removeSet[n] = struct{}{}
487		}
488		paths := make([]config.Path, 0, len(live.Paths)+len(add))
489		for _, p := range live.Paths {
490			if _, gone := removeSet[p.Name]; !gone {
491				paths = append(paths, p)
492			}
493		}
494		paths = append(paths, add...)
495		running := *live
496		running.Paths = paths
497		return &running
498	}
499	
500	// diffPaths computes, by name, which desired paths are not yet live (to add) and
501	// which live paths are no longer desired (to remove). It is a pure function so the
502	// reload diff is unit-testable without a running engine.
503	func diffPaths(live []string, desired []config.Path) (add []config.Path, remove []string) {
504		liveSet := make(map[string]struct{}, len(live))
505		for _, n := range live {
506			liveSet[n] = struct{}{}
507		}
508		desiredSet := make(map[string]struct{}, len(desired))
509		for _, p := range desired {
510			desiredSet[p.Name] = struct{}{}
511			if _, ok := liveSet[p.Name]; !ok {
512				add = append(add, p)
513			}
514		}
515		for _, n := range live {
516			if _, ok := desiredSet[n]; !ok {
517				remove = append(remove, n)
518			}
519		}
520		return add, remove
521	}
522	
523	// defaultFailbackDwell is how long a recovered higher-priority path must stay up
524	// before egress fails BACK to it, damping flap-induced thrash (T15 hysteresis).
525	// Unlike the probe-cadence/liveness thresholds (which are the shared
526	// telemetry.Default* single source of truth, D16), the failback dwell is not part
527	// of the failover-recovery budget — failover to a backup is instant — so it stays a
528	// device-local constant.
529	const defaultFailbackDwell = 5 * time.Second
530	
531	// fecConfig maps the validated [fec] config block onto the fec.Config the multipath
532	// Bind consumes (T24), or returns nil when FEC is disabled so the Bind runs the
533	// datapath with no parity plane. The ratio was already range-checked in config
534	// validation; the Bind re-validates defensively.
535	func fecConfig(f config.FEC) *fec.Config {
536		if !f.Enabled {
537			return nil
538		}
539		return &fec.Config{
540			DataShards:   f.DataShards,
541			ParityShards: f.ParityShards,
542			Deadline:     f.Deadline,
543		}
544	}
545	
546	// adaptiveFECConfig maps the [fec] block onto the adaptive controller config the multipath
547	// Bind drives (T29), or returns nil when FEC is disabled or the block did not opt into
548	// adaptive mode — in which case the Bind runs the fixed-ratio plane (T24) unchanged. The
549	// controller uses the simulation-proven default control law (internal/adaptivefec,
550	// DefaultConfig), with only the group geometry pinned to the configured ratio: DataShards
551	// (K) is the FEC data_shards and MaxParity (the parity CEILING) is the FEC parity_shards,
552	// which the receiver's decoder is built at. The Bind re-validates and cross-checks these
553	// against the FEC config defensively.
554	func adaptiveFECConfig(f config.FEC) *adaptivefec.Config {
555		if !f.Enabled || !f.Adaptive {
556			return nil
557		}
558		c := adaptivefec.DefaultConfig()
559		c.DataShards = f.DataShards
560		c.MaxParity = f.ParityShards
561		// Exactly one sizing mode is active (config load enforces mutual exclusion): in the
562		// residual-SLA mode f.SafetyFactor is 0 and f.TargetResidual drives M; in the legacy
563		// mode f.TargetResidual is 0 and f.SafetyFactor (defaulted at load) drives M.
564		c.SafetyFactor = f.SafetyFactor
565		c.TargetResidual = f.TargetResidual
566		return &c
567	}
568	
569	// buildScheduler constructs one live *telemetry.Prober per path and the P1
570	// active-backup send scheduler over them, in cfg.Paths' configured priority order
571	// (index 0 = the preferred primary). The returned probers ARE the scheduler's
572	// PathHealth sources (a *Prober is internally synchronized, satisfying the
573	// PathHealth concurrency contract — a bare *Liveness would not) and are handed to
574	// the bind so the probe transport drives the very same liveness the scheduler
575	// selects on. This replaces the T15 sched.AlwaysUp placeholder with real on-wire
576	// failover (T37).
577	func buildScheduler(cfg *config.Config, lg log.Logger) (sched.Scheduler, []*telemetry.Prober, bind.ProberFactory, error) {
578		clock := telemetry.SystemClock{}
579		proberCfg := telemetry.ProberConfig{
580			LossWindow: telemetry.DefaultLossWindow,
581			Liveness: telemetry.LivenessConfig{
582				DownAfter:        telemetry.DefaultDownAfter,
583				UpAfterSuccesses: telemetry.DefaultUpSuccesses,
584			},
585		}
586		// One random per-boot session id shared by every path's Prober (it identifies
587		// this boot, not the path): a peer restart presents a new session id that resets
588		// the surviving responder's anti-replay high-water so liveness recovers (T38, D12).
589		// A runtime-added path (T30) reuses the SAME session id, so its probes join this
590		// boot's stream and the peer's reflector adopts them without a challenge reset.
591		sessionID, err := telemetry.NewSessionID(rand.Reader)
592		if err != nil {
593			return nil, nil, nil, err
594		}
595		// newProber mints one path's Prober with the shared session/config/clock. It is
596		// the single construction point for boot-time AND runtime paths, so both measure
597		// liveness identically.
598		newProber := func(name string, id uint8) *telemetry.Prober {
599			return telemetry.NewProber(name, id, sessionID, cfg.PSK, proberCfg, clock, lg)
600		}
601		probers := make([]*telemetry.Prober, len(cfg.Paths))
602		health := make([]sched.PathHealth, len(cfg.Paths))
603		quality := make([]sched.PathQuality, len(cfg.Paths))
604		for i := range cfg.Paths {
605			probers[i] = newProber(cfg.Paths[i].Name, uint8(i))
606			health[i] = probers[i]
607			quality[i] = probers[i]
608		}
609		// Policy is a config choice: active-backup (P1, default) or the T21 weighted-
610		// aggregation policy. Both consume the SAME per-path *Prober set — a *Prober
611		// satisfies BOTH PathHealth (liveness) and PathQuality (RTT/loss Estimate) — so the
612		// probe transport drives the very liveness/quality the scheduler selects on, and the
613		// swap is behind config with no Bind change.
614		scheduler, err := selectScheduler(cfg, health, quality, clock, lg)
615		if err != nil {
616			return nil, nil, nil, err
617		}
618		return scheduler, probers, newProber, nil
619	}
620	
621	// selectScheduler builds the send scheduler the configured policy names, over the
622	// per-path health (and, for the weighted policy, quality) sources. active-backup is
623	// the P1 default; weighted is T21. The weighted knobs are validated at config load,
624	// so translating them here cannot fail on range — only NewWeighted's structural
625	// checks (which the wiring satisfies) apply.
626	func selectScheduler(cfg *config.Config, health []sched.PathHealth, quality []sched.PathQuality, clock telemetry.Clock, lg log.Logger) (sched.Scheduler, error) {
627		switch cfg.Scheduler.Policy {
628		case config.PolicyWeighted:
629			sc := cfg.Scheduler
630			return sched.NewWeighted(health, quality, sched.WeightedConfig{
631				PerPathCapacity:   sc.PerPathCapacityFPS,
632				EngageFraction:    sc.EngageFraction,
633				DisengageFraction: sc.DisengageFraction,
634				CollapseDwell:     sc.CollapseDwell,
635				LoadTau:           sc.LoadTau,
636				Pacing:            sc.PacingEnabled,
637				PacingBurst:       sc.PacingBurstFrames,
638				WeightRTTFloor:    sc.WeightRTTFloor,
639				WeightLossFloor:   sc.WeightLossFloor,
640			}, clock, lg)
641		default:
642			// active-backup (and the empty default, normalized to it at config load).
643			return sched.NewActiveBackup(
644				health,
645				sched.Config{FailbackAfter: defaultFailbackDwell},
646				clock,
647				lg,
648			)
649		}
650	}
651	
652	// Name is the created TUN interface name (for external addressing/routing).
653	func (t *Tunnel) Name() string { return t.name }
654	
655	// Wait blocks until the device is torn down (its own Close, or an unrecoverable
656	// engine error).
657	func (t *Tunnel) Wait() { <-t.dev.Wait() }
658	
659	// Close stops the probe loop, brings the device down, and releases the TUN and
660	// Bind. Idempotent. It also releases this tunnel's hold on the single-amnezia-
661	// engine-per-process guard exactly once, so a later Up may reconfigure the
662	// process-global amnezia state. Probing is stopped BEFORE the engine tears the
663	// bind's sockets down so no emission races the close.
664	func (t *Tunnel) Close() {
665		// Shut the scrape endpoint FIRST so no in-flight /metrics scrape reads the Bind while
666		// the engine tears its sockets down. reloadMu serializes this against a concurrent
667		// SIGHUP-driven rebind.
668		t.reloadMu.Lock()
669		t.stopMetricsLocked()
670		t.reloadMu.Unlock()
671		if t.stopProbes != nil {
672			t.stopProbes()
673		}
674		if t.stopReconcile != nil {
675			t.stopReconcile()
676		}
677		if t.stopHubFailover != nil {
678			t.stopHubFailover()
679		}
680		t.dev.Close()
681		t.releaseOnce.Do(func() { globalAmneziaGuard.release(t.amnezia) })
682	}
683	
684	// engineLogger adapts the amneziawg engine's logger onto wanbond's structured
685	// logger under a "wg" component. The engine is verbose only when the daemon runs
686	// at debug level; otherwise only its errors surface.
687	func engineLogger(lg log.Logger, level string) *awgdevice.Logger {
688		wg := lg.Component("wg")
689		verbosef := func(string, ...any) {}
690		if strings.EqualFold(strings.TrimSpace(level), "debug") {
691			verbosef = func(format string, args ...any) { wg.Debug(fmt.Sprintf(format, args...)) }
692		}
693		return &awgdevice.Logger{
694			Verbosef: verbosef,
695			Errorf:   func(format string, args ...any) { wg.Error(fmt.Sprintf(format, args...)) },
696		}
697	}
698	
699	// uapiConfig renders cfg into the newline-delimited UAPI set string the engine's
700	// IpcSet consumes. Keys are lowercase hex (UAPI's on-the-wire encoding), NOT the
701	// base64 form the TOML carries. Amnezia obfuscation keys are emitted only when the
702	// block is configured; an all-zero Amnezia block leaves the engine in plain
703	// WireGuard mode. The same amnezia parameters are applied on BOTH roles (edge and
704	// concentrator) as defense-in-depth — they must match end to end for the handshake
705	// to succeed (config validation makes each end specify a complete profile, D1).
706	func uapiConfig(cfg *config.Config) (string, error) {
707		var b strings.Builder
708	
709		priv := cfg.WireGuard.PrivateKey.Bytes()
710		fmt.Fprintf(&b, "private_key=%s\n", hex.EncodeToString(priv[:]))
711		if cfg.Role == config.RoleConcentrator {
712			fmt.Fprintf(&b, "listen_port=%d\n", cfg.WireGuard.ListenPort)
713		}
714		writeAmnezia(&b, cfg.Amnezia)
715	
716		for i, peer := range cfg.WireGuard.Peers {
717			pub := peer.PublicKey.Bytes()
718			fmt.Fprintf(&b, "public_key=%s\n", hex.EncodeToString(pub[:]))
719			if len(peer.Endpoints) > 0 {
720				// Endpoints[0] is the active/primary concentrator (Q18 ordered-endpoint
721				// active-standby); it is populated from either the legacy single
722				// `endpoint` field or the new ordered `endpoints` list (config.Peer.
723				// resolveEndpoints), so both forms bring up the tunnel identically here.
724				// Switching to a standby endpoint on hub loss is T57's job, not this
725				// initial UAPI render.
726				fmt.Fprintf(&b, "endpoint=%s\n", peer.Endpoints[0])
727				// A keepalive keeps the edge->concentrator session warm and lets the
728				// concentrator relearn the edge endpoint after a NAT rebind; only the
729				// initiating (edge) side sets it.
730				fmt.Fprintf(&b, "persistent_keepalive_interval=%d\n", keepaliveSeconds)
731			}
732			if len(peer.AllowedIPs) == 0 {
733				return "", fmt.Errorf("peer %d (%s): at least one allowed_ip is required", i, hex.EncodeToString(pub[:8]))
734			}
735			for _, cidr := range peer.AllowedIPs {
736				fmt.Fprintf(&b, "allowed_ip=%s\n", cidr)
737			}
738		}
739		return b.String(), nil
740	}
741	
742	// keepaliveSeconds is the edge's persistent-keepalive interval.
743	const keepaliveSeconds = 25
744	
745	// writeAmnezia emits the nine amneziawg obfuscation UAPI keys, but only when the
746	// block is configured. When amnezia is unused, wanbond leaves the engine's
747	// PROCESS-GLOBAL message-type state untouched (see the amneziaGuard invariant).
748	// Config validation guarantees a configured block is complete and self-consistent
749	// (D1), and applyDefaults has already filled the standard magic headers (1..4)
750	// when they were omitted, so the emitted set never carries an h*=0 sentinel.
751	func writeAmnezia(b *strings.Builder, a config.Amnezia) {
752		if !a.Configured() {
753			return
754		}
755		fmt.Fprintf(b, "jc=%d\njmin=%d\njmax=%d\ns1=%d\ns2=%d\nh1=%d\nh2=%d\nh3=%d\nh4=%d\n",
756			a.Jc, a.Jmin, a.Jmax, a.S1, a.S2, a.H1, a.H2, a.H3, a.H4)
757	}

content.text:
I now have thorough grounding. Confirmed premises:
- `config.Config.PSK` is a single top-level key (config.go:42); `config.Peer` has no PSK field; `validate()` requires the top-level `psk`; peers are already plural.
- `device.Up` builds ONE `bind.NewMultipath(cfg.Paths, cfg.PSK, scheduler, probers, ...)` for the whole process — a single Bind, single scheduler, single prober set, single Reflector keyed on the one PSK (multipath.go:402). The whole datapath is structurally single-peer.
- `handleInbound` learns a path's return remote ONLY from an authenticated PROBE (D9/D11), never from unauthenticated DATA — so an authenticated path→peer binding mechanism already partially exists to build on.
- `reseq` is a single outer-seq-keyed instance and already carries a `Rebaseline`/resync guard for the existing T57/Q18 edge-side single-active hub-failover.

This is rule 2(a): fresh goal, no open questions, planning still blocked. Filing the first batch of clarifying questions.

fields.question: Confirm the scope boundary: is this goal STRICTLY concentrator-side multi-peer (one concentrator process terminating N distinct edges, each a distinct WireGuard peer/pubkey), with NO change to the edge side? Specifically, (a) is edge-side simultaneous aggregation across multiple DISTINCT concentrators explicitly out of scope (it is different from the already-shipped T57/Q18 single-active-hub ordered-endpoint failover), and (b) is the existing single-edge NAT-roaming case (one peer whose source rebinds) considered ALREADY handled, so 'multi-peer' means genuinely distinct peers, not the roaming of one?
fields.context: Grounded: device.Up builds ONE bind.NewMultipath(cfg.Paths, cfg.PSK, ...) for the whole process; the edge already has T57 hub-failover (config.Peer.Endpoints ordered list, reseq.Rebaseline, startHubFailover in device.go) which is single-ACTIVE, not simultaneous aggregation. config.validate already accepts >=1 [[wireguard.peers]] and device.uapiConfig ranges over all peers, but the Bind/reseq/scheduler are singletons. The scope answer decides whether ANY edge-side work is in the plan or the plan is concentrator-only de-singletoning.
suggestions: Concentrator-only multi-peer; edge unchanged; edge-multi-hub and single-edge-roaming both out of scope
Concentrator multi-peer PLUS edge-side simultaneous multi-concentrator aggregation
Something else (describe)
fields.recommendation: Concentrator-only multi-peer for this goal; edge-side simultaneous multi-concentrator aggregation is a separate feature; single-edge roaming is already handled and is not what 'multi-peer' means here.

fields.question: Adopt a PER-PEER PSK (move psk into [[wireguard.peers]]) as the authenticated path->peer demux enabler? If yes, pin the exact config schema and back-compat rule: (a) does the top-level `psk` REMAIN fully supported as the single-peer default (so every existing single-peer config keeps working byte-for-byte), with a per-peer `psk` under [[wireguard.peers]] only REQUIRED once >1 peer is configured? (b) does validate REQUIRE the per-peer psks to be pairwise DISTINCT when >1 peer (equal psks would defeat authenticated demux)? (c) is the model symmetric on the edge (each edge configured with the single psk that matches its concentrator-side peer entry), i.e. no edge schema change beyond the value it already sets?
fields.context: Grounded: config.Config.PSK is a single top-level Key (config.go:42), config.validate requires it (line 844), config.Peer has PublicKey/Endpoint(s)/AllowedIPs and NO psk field. multipath.NewMultipath takes one psk and builds ONE frame.Codec + telemetry.NewReflector(psk) + per-path telemetry.NewProber(...,cfg.PSK,...). The outer PSK-HMAC PROBE/CONTROL plane is the only authenticated signal below the crypto layer, so a per-peer PSK is what lets an authenticated PROBE identify WHICH peer a path belongs to. This decision drives internal/config schema+validation AND how the Bind is de-singletoned (map keyed by peer, each peerState with its own psk-derived codec/reflector).
suggestions: Per-peer psk under [[wireguard.peers]]; top-level psk stays the single-peer default; per-peer required + pairwise-distinct only when >1 peer; edge unchanged
Per-peer psk ALWAYS required (drop top-level psk) — a clean break, no single-peer back-compat
Keep one deployment-wide psk + some non-PSK demux (describe how it stays unforgeable)
fields.recommendation: Per-peer psk under [[wireguard.peers]] as the enabler; keep top-level psk as the single-peer back-compat default; require per-peer psks present and pairwise-distinct when >1 peer; no edge schema change (the edge already sets exactly the psk matching its concentrator peer).

fields.question: Is a DATA/PARITY outer-frame WIRE-FORMAT change acceptable for this goal, or is 'no wire change' a hard requirement? The chosen direction (authenticated path->peer binding) should need NO wire change: DATA/PARITY stay unauthenticated with no peer id, and demux is purely by the authenticated path->peer binding table. Confirm that (a) adding a peer-id field to the DATA header is explicitly REJECTED (it would be spoofable and reintroduce the cross-peer resequencer-injection DoS invariant-4 forbids), and (b) the plan must preserve byte-for-byte wire compatibility with already-deployed single-peer edges.
fields.context: Grounded: frame.Data carries OuterSeq/PathID/FECGroup/FECIndex/Flags and NO peer id; DATA/PARITY are unauthenticated by design (frame.go wire-model comment, invariant 4); only PROBE/CONTROL carry a PSK-HMAC tag. reseq's whole discontinuity/resync guard assumes DATA is forgeable. A wire change would ripple into frame.DataOverhead, mtu.go InnerMTU sizing, FEC shard coding (OuterSeq||Payload), and cross-version compat. Answering this fixes whether internal/frame is in the refactor surface at all.
suggestions: No wire change; DATA/PARITY unchanged; demux purely via authenticated path->peer binding (peer-id-in-DATA rejected); keep single-peer wire compat
A wire change IS acceptable if planning shows it is necessary (describe the compat story)
fields.recommendation: No wire change. DATA/PARITY stay unauthenticated and peer-id-free; the peer is resolved by the authenticated path->peer binding, not by any outer-frame field. This preserves invariant 4 and wire compat with deployed edges.

fields.question: How is an inbound path/source attributed to a peer for the VERY FIRST frames, before any authenticated PROBE has bound it (and before the WG handshake completes)? Two sub-decisions: (1) Bootstrap policy for early DATA on an unbound source — GATE (drop DATA until an authenticated PROBE under some peer's psk binds the source->peer, relying on WG handshake/keepalive retransmit), or QUARANTINE it in a provisional per-source resequencer that is adopted once the binding resolves? (2) Peer identification for an unbound authenticated frame — is it acceptable to TRIAL-DECODE an unbound PROBE against each configured peer's psk (O(peers) HMAC verifies) to discover which peer it belongs to, or is a cheaper binding hint required?
fields.context: Grounded: multipath.handleInbound today learns a path's return remote ONLY from an authenticated PROBE (Probe case: ps.setRemote(srcAP)), never from DATA (the D9/D11 fix), and reflects peer probes via telemetry.Reflector. With per-peer psk each peerState has its own psk-derived Codec, so an unbound source's frame must be trial-decoded across peers' codecs to identify the peer. Trial-decode is an O(peers) cost + a potential CPU-DoS surface on spoofed unbound sources (ties to the resource-limit and threat-model questions). This decision shapes the demux table programming in device.go and the Bind receive path.
suggestions: Gate DATA until an authenticated PROBE binds source->peer; identify the peer by trial-decoding the PROBE across peer psks (bounded by max-peers)
Provisional quarantine resequencer per unbound source, adopted on first authenticated PROBE
A cheaper binding hint is required (describe)
fields.recommendation: Gate DATA on an unbound source until an authenticated PROBE binds source->peer (WG retransmits cover the brief gap); identify the peer by trial-decoding the unbound PROBE across configured peer psks, with the cost bounded by the max-peers cap and unauthenticated floods dropped cheaply.

fields.question: When an edge's source rebinds (NAT/roaming) so a path arrives from a NEW source address, how must the path re-bind to the SAME peer without a window where its frames misroute into another peer's resequencer? Is it acceptable that re-binding a moved source to its peer happens ONLY on a fresh authenticated PROBE (under that peer's psk) from the new source — accepting a brief drop/no-route window for that path's DATA until the PROBE re-binds it (covered by WG retransmit and the other still-bound paths of the same bonded edge) — or is a stronger continuity guarantee required?
fields.context: Grounded: today a single virt is pinned once to the first learned source (virtualEndpoint), and per-path remote is re-learned on each authenticated PROBE (handleInbound Probe case). The existing T16 re-roam and D11 machinery already re-learn a path's remote from authenticated probes. In the multi-peer world the RISK is that DATA from a moved source, arriving before its re-binding PROBE, could be attributed to the wrong peer's resequencer (cross-peer contamination). Gating re-bind on an authenticated PROBE keeps the binding unforgeable but opens a small window. This decides the roaming/re-bind logic in the demux table.
suggestions: Re-bind a moved source to its peer ONLY on a fresh authenticated PROBE from the new source; unbound/other DATA from that source is dropped (not misrouted) until then
Require a stronger zero-window continuity guarantee (describe the mechanism)
fields.recommendation: Re-bind on the authenticated PROBE only; until the moved source re-binds, its DATA is dropped rather than attributed to any peer (never misrouted). WG retransmit and the edge's other still-bound paths cover the brief window, mirroring the existing D11/T16 re-learn discipline.

fields.question: Pin the concentrator resource-limit model: (a) is there a CONFIGURED max-peers cap, and what default (the per-peer footprint is a ~2048-frame resequencer ring PLUS per-peer FEC send/recv state PLUS per-peer scheduler/probers/reflector, so N peers multiply memory)? (b) what is the eviction policy for idle/dead peers — evict when the WG session is torn down / liveness has been DOWN past a timeout, or never (static peer set only)? (c) on cap exhaustion, REJECT a new peer's bootstrap, or evict the idlest? Note: is the concentrator peer set STATIC (only the configured [[wireguard.peers]] ever bind) or can peers appear dynamically within that configured set?
fields.context: Grounded: resequencerWindow=2048 (multipath.go), and each peer needs its own resequencer (atomic.Pointer today), fecSend/fecRecv, scheduler, prober set, and reflector — all currently process-singletons. The configured peer set is bounded by [[wireguard.peers]] (config), so 'max peers' may simply be len(peers); but the demux/provisional state for UNbound sources needs its own bound (DoS). This decision sets the peerState map sizing, the eviction lifecycle wired from device.go peer events, and the backpressure branch.
suggestions: Static configured peer set only (cap = number of [[wireguard.peers]]); per-peer state torn down when that peer's WG session/liveness goes away; provisional unbound-source state separately capped
Configured max_peers cap with a default (state the number) + idle-eviction timeout
No cap / no eviction (reject if unsure)
fields.recommendation: Peers are the STATIC configured [[wireguard.peers]] set, so the steady-state cap is that count; size per-peer state lazily and tear it down when a peer's WG session/liveness is gone; cap the PROVISIONAL unbound-source demux state separately (small, drop-on-exhaustion) to bound the bootstrap DoS.

fields.question: State the target THREAT MODEL for the path->peer binding so acceptance can test it: (1) With distinct per-peer PSKs, is the required guarantee 'a malicious edge that knows ITS OWN psk can disrupt ONLY its own tunnel, never another peer's resequencing/FEC/scheduling' (i.e. full cross-peer isolation, since it cannot forge an authenticated PROBE under a victim's psk)? (2) For an attacker with NO valid psk who floods spoofed/unbound source addresses, is the accepted bound 'unauthenticated frames are dropped cheaply and provisional demux state is capped, so the flood cannot exhaust memory/CPU or evict a live peer' — accepting only degraded bootstrap latency, never cross-peer corruption?
fields.context: Grounded: DATA/PARITY are unforgeable-DATA by design (invariant 4); only an authenticated PROBE (PSK-HMAC, with monotonic anti-replay + the T38/D12 session challenge in telemetry) can bind a source to a peer. So cross-peer injection requires the victim's psk. The residual surfaces are (i) trial-decode CPU on unbound floods and (ii) provisional-state exhaustion / live-peer eviction — both tie to the demux-bootstrap and resource-limit questions. A written threat model turns the e2e 'one edge's loss/restart does not corrupt another' test into concrete adversary cases.
suggestions: Full cross-peer isolation under distinct psks; no-psk floods bounded to degraded bootstrap only (never corruption or live-peer eviction)
A weaker/different guarantee (describe)
fields.recommendation: Target full cross-peer isolation: with distinct per-peer psks a peer can disrupt only itself; an attacker without a valid psk is limited to (bounded, capped) bootstrap-latency degradation and can neither corrupt another peer's stream nor evict a live peer.

fields.question: For /metrics: add a per-peer label to the wanbond_path_* (and the per-peer resequencer/FEC) series so an operator can attribute traffic/loss/recovery to a specific edge? If yes, what is the label KEYED on — a stable config-assigned peer name (recommended, human-readable, bounded), or the WG public key (globally stable but opaque and higher-cardinality-looking)? Confirm the cardinality increase (series multiply by peer count) is acceptable given it is bounded by the max-peers/static peer set, and whether existing single-peer series must stay label-compatible (e.g. omit the peer label, or emit a default, when only one peer).
fields.context: Grounded: metrics.Source (newMetricsSource) reads per-PATH counters (txBytes/rxBytes atomics on pathState, prober RTT/loss, resequencer Stats) off the single Bind; there is no peer dimension today. Per-peer isolation makes per-peer scrape data the operator's primary signal ('is edge A's tunnel healthy independently of edge B'). config.Peer has no name field today, so keying on a peer name may require a small schema add (peer name/id). This decides the metrics.Source shape and any config surface for a peer identifier.
suggestions: Add a `peer` label keyed on a config-assigned peer name (add a name/id field to [[wireguard.peers]]); single-peer configs keep back-compatible series
Key the `peer` label on the WG public key (no new config field)
No per-peer labels for this goal (aggregate only)
fields.recommendation: Add a per-peer `peer` label keyed on a config-assigned peer name (small [[wireguard.peers]] name field); cardinality is bounded by the static peer set; keep single-peer series back-compatible (omit the label or emit a stable default when only one peer).

Filed 8 clarifying questions (Q21–Q28) under M18, each linked to `goals:G4` and left `open`. The goal stays in `clarifying` — the server gates the next phase on these being answered. No plan emitted, no decision locked, per the clarify-first round.

### Session summary
- **Did:** Ran the DEFAULT single-planner step for fresh goal G4 (rule 2(a)): grounded read-only against the cited code, then filed the first batch of clarifying questions.
- **Achieved:** Verified every premise the goal cites, then created Q21–Q28 (open, under M18, `ledgerRefs: ["goals:G4"]`) covering: scope boundary; per-peer-PSK model + config schema + single-peer back-compat; wire-format-change acceptability; demux bootstrapping (gate-vs-quarantine + trial-decode-across-peer-PSKs); roaming re-bind; concentrator resource limits/eviction/backpressure; threat model for the path→peer binding; per-peer /metrics labels. Goal left in `clarifying` (no transition performed).
- **Discovered:** The premises hold in the tree. `config.Config.PSK` is a single top-level key (config.go:42), `config.Peer` has no PSK field, and `config.validate` requires the top-level psk — so per-peer PSK is a real schema change. `device.Up` builds exactly ONE `bind.NewMultipath(cfg.Paths, cfg.PSK, ...)` with one scheduler/prober set/Reflector — the datapath is structurally single-peer even though `[[wireguard.peers]]` and `uapiConfig` already iterate all peers. Two useful existing hooks the plan can build on: (1) `multipath.handleInbound` already learns a path's return remote ONLY from an authenticated PROBE (D9/D11), the seed of an authenticated path→peer binding; (2) `reseq.Rebaseline` + the resync/corroboration guard already exist for the T57/Q18 edge-side single-active hub-failover, which is distinct from this goal's concentrator multi-peer aggregation.
- **Issues:** None; blocked on the user answering Q21–Q28 before a fine-grained plan can be written. Note the questions are interdependent (demux-bootstrap Q24, resource-limits Q26, threat-model Q27 form one cluster; per-peer-PSK Q22 and metrics Q28 both may touch a new per-peer config field), so the answers should be read together at plan time.
